The arc: personal productivity → enterprise data → deployment → strategy
XYZ Corp Custom Chatbot: a custom chatbot for a $500M distributor — starting in Week 2
By Session 8: a concrete AI strategy for your organization



Before we dive in, let me quickly recap what you saw in the pre-program video. You learned about the three tools we will use — Claude in the browser, Claude Desktop for working with your own files, and Claude Code which I will use to build things in later sessions. You saw the overall arc of the course: we start with personal productivity, move to enterprise data in Week 2, then cover deployment and governance, and finish with strategy. And you got a preview of the XYZ Corp Custom Chatbot, the simulated company environment we will use starting in Session 3. Today, though, it is all about you and your immediate productivity.

title: Session 1: What AI Can Do for You
text:
The “Why Now”
In February 2026, AI agents wiped $2 trillion from software company valuations.

So why does this matter right now? Let me set the context. In February 2026, something remarkable happened. AI agents — software that can understand plain English and take action — triggered the largest repricing in software history. Two trillion dollars in market value disappeared in a matter of weeks. (Source: aggregate market cap decline across the BVP Nasdaq Emerging Cloud Index and major SaaS constituents, February 2026.) The market sent a very clear signal: AI can replace large categories of enterprise software. Whether or not you agree with the market’s verdict, the technology behind it is real, and it is moving fast.

title: Session 1: What AI Can Do for You
text:
The SAASpocalypse

“The AI agent revolution is wreaking havoc on traditional SaaS valuations. Investors are betting that intelligent agents will replace the click-heavy workflows that enterprise software companies have spent decades building.”


— CNBC, “Software stocks face worst rout in a decade as AI agents threaten business models,” February 2026


The press called it the SAASpocalypse. CNBC, TechCrunch, and every major financial outlet covered it. The thesis was simple: if an AI agent can do what a human does inside Salesforce, or SAP, or ServiceNow — querying data, generating reports, automating workflows — then why pay per-seat license fees for the software itself? The market decided that the answer, for many categories, is: you will not need to.

title: Session 1: What AI Can Do for You
text:
The Damage


−31% Salesforce


−32% Adobe


−35% Atlassian



Here are some of the numbers. Salesforce, the world’s largest CRM company, lost 31 percent of its value. Adobe, which dominates creative software, dropped 32 percent. Atlassian, the maker of Jira and Confluence — tools used by virtually every software team — fell 35 percent. These are not small companies. These are pillars of the enterprise software industry, and the market is telling them their moats may be thinner than they thought. To be fair, SaaS stocks saw a similar sell-off in 2022 driven by rising rates, and many recovered. Recovery is possible here too. But the structural shift this time is different — agents threaten the per-seat pricing model itself, not just near-term growth expectations.

title: Session 1: What AI Can Do for You
text:
What AI Is Replacing


The Interface Layer

Click-heavy workflows
Form-filling and navigation
Manual data entry across screens
“Training” users on software



Workflow Automation

Rule-based routing
Report scheduling
Template-driven outputs
Repetitive analysis tasks



Per-Seat Economics

$150-300/user/month SaaS fees
Shelfware (paid but unused)
Feature bloat nobody asked for
Integration tax between tools




So what exactly is AI replacing? Three things. First, the interface layer — all those clicks, forms, and navigation that enterprise software forces you through. If you can just say what you want in English, the UI becomes unnecessary. Second, workflow automation — rules-based routing, report scheduling, template-driven outputs. AI handles these natively. Third, the per-seat economics model itself. Companies pay 150 to 300 dollars per user per month for SaaS tools, and much of that capacity goes unused. AI collapses the cost structure.

title: Session 1: What AI Can Do for You
text:
What Survives
Data
The systems of record. The databases. The institutional knowledge embedded in your CRM, ERP, and HR systems.
AI does not replace your data — it makes it accessible.

But here is what does not get replaced: data. Your CRM still holds your customer relationships. Your ERP still tracks your supply chain. Your HR system still manages your people. AI does not replace those systems of record. What it does is make the data inside them dramatically more accessible. Instead of needing a BI team to build a dashboard, you ask a question in English. That is the shift — and it is why we spend the second half of this course on enterprise data. If someone pushes back — “SaaS stocks have crashed before and recovered” — that is a fair point. The difference this time is the economic model: agents eliminate per-seat pricing, not just automate workflows. But acknowledge the uncertainty.

The 90-day pilot plan




These are distinct but related. Personal productivity is Week 1. Corporate implementation is Weeks 2–4. The skills from the first feed directly into the second.


This course is organized around two distinct but related questions. The first is personal: how can I use AI plus code to get the data I need, analyze it, and generate deliverables? Charts, documents, Excel workbooks, PowerPoint decks — the things you produce every day. That is what Sessions 1 and 2 are about, and those skills are available to you right now, today, with no IT department involvement.
The second question is organizational: what should my company be doing to implement AI-assisted data handling and analysis? How do you connect AI to enterprise databases? Where does the code run? Who governs access? How do you verify the output? That is what Sessions 3 through 8 are about.
These two questions are related because the skills you develop for personal productivity — iteration, critique, calibrated trust — are exactly the skills you need to evaluate and govern corporate AI systems. The personal becomes the foundation for the organizational.

Before AI transforms your organization, it can transform your workday.

We start with question one — personal productivity. Before AI transforms your organization, it can transform your workday. The gains are available right now, with no IT approval needed. That is where we begin. Keep the opening brisk — the demo is where engagement starts.

title: Session 1: What AI Can Do for You
text:
The Key Framing

AI is a fast junior analyst.
It drafts. You direct and verify.
It is not replacing your judgment — it is accelerating your output.


I want you to hold onto this framing throughout the entire course. AI is a fast junior analyst. It can produce a first draft of almost anything in seconds. But like any junior analyst, it needs direction, it sometimes makes mistakes, and you are the one who decides whether the output is good enough to use. Your judgment, your domain expertise, your understanding of the audience — that is what turns an AI draft into a real deliverable.

title: Session 1: What AI Can Do for You
section: How AI + Code + Data Work Together
text:
How AI + Code + Data Work Together

Now let me explain something important about how these systems actually work. Understanding this architecture will help you make sense of everything we cover in this course — from personal productivity today to enterprise deployment in later sessions.

title: Session 1: What AI Can Do for You
text:
Three Moving Pieces


The AI Brain

The large language model (LLM)
Usually in the cloud — hosted by Anthropic, OpenAI, Google, etc.
Can also run on-premise for sensitive data (Session 5)
Understands your question
Generates text, code, and plans



The Data

Could be on your computer (spreadsheets, local files)
Could be in SaaS provider storage (Salesforce, Workday, NetSuite)
Could be on company-managed servers (on-prem or cloud)
Where data lives determines security requirements



Code Execution

Something has to run the Python, SQL, or scripts the AI writes
Could run on the LLM provider’s hardware (Claude.ai sandbox)
Could run on company servers (Docker, on-prem)
Could run on your computer (Claude Desktop, terminal)




There are three moving pieces in every AI system, and understanding where each one lives is the key to understanding the whole course. First, the AI brain — the large language model. This is usually in the cloud, hosted by the provider — Anthropic, OpenAI, Google. But it does not have to be. For organizations with sensitive data, you can run an LLM on your own servers — on-premise. The tradeoff is cost and model quality versus full data control. We will cover that decision in detail in Session 5. Second, the data. This could be on your own computer — a spreadsheet, a PDF. It could be in SaaS provider storage — your Salesforce CRM, your Workday HR system. Or it could be on company-managed servers. Where your data lives determines the security requirements. Third, code execution. When the AI writes Python or SQL to analyze data, something has to actually run that code. It could run on the LLM provider’s hardware — that is what happens when you use Claude.ai’s sandbox. It could run on your company’s servers. Or it could run on your own computer. Each combination has different implications for security, cost, and capability.

There are three ways you will experience AI plus code plus data in this course. Option 1: on your own computer. I will provide install files so you can run everything locally — Python, Claude Code, your own data. This requires an Anthropic Pro account, which is not provided by the course, so this is optional for those who want the deepest hands-on experience. You get full control and full privacy — nothing leaves your machine except what goes to the AI brain in the cloud.
Option 2: the AI+Code Lab. This is the zero-friction option. No software to install, no cost to you. You log into a shared cloud environment where you have your own account. It includes Claude Code and pre-loaded exercises. Data and code execution happen on the Lab. The API key is provided. This simulates working on your own computer — the experience is the same, but there is zero setup. This simulates how AI would work in a corporate deployment: the data is not on your machine, the code does not run on your machine, and the AI is in the cloud. This is the architecture most of your companies will use in production.

Here is how the three options map to the architecture we just discussed. In all three cases, the AI brain is in the cloud — that never changes. What changes is where your data lives and where the code runs. On your own computer, both are local. On the AI+Code Lab, both are on a shared cloud environment you log into. In the XYZ Corp Custom Chatbot, both are remote — databases and code execution are on servers you do not control. That last option is what most corporate deployments look like. And the questions it raises — who can access the data, where does it travel, who governs the output — are exactly what we spend Sessions 5 and 6 on.

title: Session 1: What AI Can Do for You
text:
Hands-On: Your First Artifacts

Time for you to try it yourselves. We have three guided exercises, each demonstrating a different type of artifact Claude can create. I will walk you through the first one, and then you will have time to work through all three at your own pace. Open claude.ai in your browser now if you have not already.

title: Session 1: What AI Can Do for You
text:
Exercise 1: Build a Chart

Instructions

Paste the sample dataset (provided in chat) into Claude.ai
Ask: “Create a bar chart comparing Q1 through Q4 revenue by product line”
Iterate: “Change the colors to blue and gray” → “Add data labels” → “Switch to a line chart” → “Make it a stacked bar chart”
Notice the interactive artifact — hover, filter, explore



The point: You are not learning to code. You are learning to direct. Tell it what you want, see the result, refine with plain English.


Exercise one is about visualization. I have pasted a sample dataset into the chat — quarterly revenue for three product lines. Copy it into Claude.ai and ask for a bar chart. But do not stop at the first version. This is where the real skill is. Ask it to change colors, add labels, switch chart types. Try “make it a stacked bar chart” or “add a trend line.” Each iteration takes seconds, and you are learning the most important skill in working with AI: directing and refining. You are not coding. You are describing what you want and adjusting until it is right.

title: Session 1: What AI Can Do for You
text:
Exercise 2: Build an Interactive Calculator

Instructions

Ask Claude: “Build me an interactive break-even analysis calculator”
It should have inputs for: fixed costs, variable cost per unit, selling price per unit
Iterate: “Add a chart showing profit/loss across different volumes” → “Add a sensitivity table” → “Make the inputs sliders instead of text boxes”
Try your own: ROI calculator, loan amortization, pricing model



Exercise two is about interactive tools. Ask Claude to build a break-even calculator. It will create a working application right in the chat — with input fields, calculations, and output. Then iterate: ask for a chart, a sensitivity table, sliders instead of text boxes. The artifact is fully interactive, and you can keep refining it. If break-even does not fit your work, try an ROI calculator, a loan amortization schedule, or a pricing model. The point is that Claude can build functional tools, not just static documents.

title: Session 1: What AI Can Do for You
text:
Exercise 3: Draft an Executive Summary

Instructions

Open the briefing document (provided) — a two-page quarterly performance update
Paste it into Claude and ask: “Write a one-page executive summary for the CEO”
Iterate: “Make it half the length” → “Lead with the biggest risk” → “Add a recommendation section” → “Change the tone to be more direct”
Compare your iterations — notice how specific direction produces specific improvement



Exercise three is about written documents. I have provided a two-page quarterly briefing document. Paste it into Claude and ask for a one-page executive summary for the CEO. Then iterate — and pay close attention to how the specificity of your direction determines the quality of the output. Saying “make it better” gets you almost nothing. Saying “lead with the biggest risk and cut the background section” gets you a dramatically different and usually better document. This is the core skill: specific direction produces specific improvement.

title: Session 1: What AI Can Do for You
text:
Open Exploration
Bring something from your actual work.

Now I want you to go beyond the exercises. For the next ten minutes, try Claude with something from your actual work. A dataset you have been meaning to analyze. A document you need to draft. A summary you have been putting off. A comparison you need to make. The exercises showed you what is possible — now apply it to something real. If you produce something interesting, I will ask you to share it with the group. And if you get stuck, raise your hand or drop a message in the chat.

title: Session 1: What AI Can Do for You
text:
What to Try


Analyze

Paste a dataset
Ask for trends
Request comparisons
Generate charts



Draft

Email to a client
Meeting summary
Project proposal
Status update



Summarize

Long report → key points
Article → executive brief
Meeting notes → action items
Research → decision memo



Build

Calculator or estimator
Comparison matrix
Decision framework
Checklist or rubric




Here are some ideas if you need a starting point. You can analyze — paste a dataset and ask for trends, comparisons, or charts. You can draft — an email, a meeting summary, a project proposal. You can summarize — take a long document and ask for key points or an executive brief. Or you can build — a calculator, a comparison matrix, a decision framework. All of these are things Claude handles well, and all of them are things that typically take you 30 minutes to an hour but take Claude about 30 seconds for a first draft.

title: Session 1: What AI Can Do for You
text:
Breakout Discussion
Groups of 3-4

Let me move you into breakout rooms now. You will be in groups of three or four. I have three questions for you to discuss, and I will ask each group to share one insight when we come back together.

title: Session 1: What AI Can Do for You
text:
Discussion Questions


What surprised you?

What did AI do better than expected?
Where did it fall short?
What was the quality like?



What would you use tomorrow?

Which task from your real work fits best?
What would you delegate to AI first?
Where is the biggest time savings?



What patterns do you see?

What tasks follow a repeatable pattern?
Where do you spend time on structure, not substance?
What recurring work could AI accelerate?




Here are your three discussion questions. First: what surprised you? Both on the upside and the downside. Second: what would you actually use this for tomorrow? Not in theory — in practice, this week, at your desk. And third: what patterns do you see? What tasks in your work follow a repeatable pattern where you spend more time on structure and formatting than on actual thinking? Those are the tasks where AI delivers the most value. You have about 15 minutes. Each group should be ready to share one insight.

title: Session 1: What AI Can Do for You
text:
Group Debrief
Each group: share your one best insight.

Welcome back. Let me hear from each group — one insight, one thing that came up in your discussion that you think the rest of the class should hear. We will go quickly around the room.

title: Session 1: What AI Can Do for You
text:
The Maker/Checker Preview


The Maker

AI generates the first draft
Fast, tireless, format-flexible
No domain expertise
No sense of stakes or audience
Will confidently produce wrong answers



The Checker

You verify, refine, and approve
Domain knowledge and judgment
Understands the audience
Knows what “wrong” costs
The irreplaceable human role




Before we wrap up, I want to plant a seed that will grow throughout this entire course. Everything AI produces needs verification. Everything. The question is not whether to check — it is how much to check. And the answer depends on the stakes. This is the maker/checker framework. AI is the maker — fast, tireless, format-flexible, but with no domain expertise and no sense of what getting it wrong would cost. You are the checker — you bring judgment, domain knowledge, and an understanding of your audience. We will formalize this framework next session.

title: Session 1: What AI Can Do for You
text:
The Spectrum of Trust


🟢 Low Stakes Personal notes, brainstorming, exploration


🟡 Medium Stakes Team decks, internal reports, summaries


🔴 High Stakes Board decks, regulatory filings, public docs


🎯 Your Judgment Match verification effort to consequences



Think of it as a spectrum. Green: low stakes — personal notes, brainstorming, quick explorations. A fast sanity check is enough. Yellow: medium stakes — team presentations, internal reports, meeting summaries. You need to verify numbers and check that the framing is accurate. Red: high stakes — board decks, regulatory filings, anything public-facing. Every claim, every number, every source needs independent verification. And the skill you are developing is judgment — knowing where each output falls on this spectrum and calibrating your verification effort accordingly. One important thing to keep in mind: AI can be confidently wrong. It will never say “I am not sure” — it will present an incorrect answer with the same polish as a correct one. Sessions 5 and 6 will show you specific examples of this and how to guard against it. Next session, we formalize this into a framework you can apply to every AI interaction.

title: Session 1: What AI Can Do for You
text:
Homework

Here is your homework for this week. It is designed to build on what you did today and give you real experience to bring to our discussion next session.

title: Session 1: What AI Can Do for You
text:
Your Assignment This Week

Homework — Due Session 2

Use Claude.ai for at least 3 real work tasks — a chart, a document draft, an analysis, a summary, whatever fits your workflow. Screenshot or save the outputs.
For each task, note:

What was good about the output?
What needed editing?
How long would it have taken without AI?

Bring to Session 2: your best example and your worst example.



Three things. First, use Claude for at least three real work tasks this week. Not exercises — real work. A chart you actually need, a document you actually have to draft, an analysis you actually have to do. Save or screenshot the outputs. Second, for each task, write down what was good, what needed editing, and how long it would have taken you without AI. This reflection is important — it builds your calibration for when AI helps and when it does not. Third, bring your best example and your worst example to Session 2. We will start next session with a debrief, and the best learning comes from both the successes and the failures.

title: Session 1: What AI Can Do for You
text:
Next Session Preview


Session 2: Iterate, Critique, Trust

The art of iteration — turning mediocre drafts into great ones
The maker/checker framework formalized
Skills and structured prompts for consistent output
Build a reusable workflow for your recurring tasks



The Course Ahead

Session 3: AI meets your enterprise data
Session 4: From data to deliverables
Sessions 5-6: Deployment and governance
Sessions 7-8: Strategy and capstone




Next session we go deeper. You will learn the art of iteration — how to take a mediocre first draft and turn it into something genuinely useful through specific, directed feedback. We will formalize the maker/checker framework. And you will build a reusable workflow for a recurring task in your work — something you can use every week, not just once. After that, in Session 3, we connect AI to enterprise data for the first time, and the rest of the course builds from there. But it all starts with the personal productivity skills you practiced today. See you next session.

title: Session 3: AI Meets Your Data
section: From Personal Productivity to Enterprise Data
text:
From Personal Productivity to Enterprise Data

This section divider opens the bridge segment (minutes 0-8). You are transitioning the class from “personal AI” mode — where they spent a week generating charts, documents, and workflows with Claude — into “enterprise AI” mode, where the same technology connects to corporate databases and company documents. Let the title land, then advance.

title: Session 3: AI Meets Your Data
text:
The Shift

You have spent a week using AI for personal productivity — charts, documents, workflows. Now we scale up. The same AI that drafts your memos can also query your company’s databases and search your company’s documents.


But enterprise data is scattered across 5, 10, sometimes 20 systems — and trapped in policies, contracts, and board minutes nobody can search. Today we see what that looks like and what AI can do about it.


Say: “You have spent a week using AI for personal productivity — charts, documents, workflows. You have iterated, critiqued, and built reusable prompts. Now we scale up. The same AI that drafts your memos can also query your company’s databases and search your company’s documents.”
“By the end of this session, you will have queried enterprise databases in plain English, watched AI merge data across systems that don’t talk to each other, seen AI answer questions from company documents with page-number citations, and started mapping your own company’s data landscape. Let’s jump right in.”
Transition: advance to the cold open.

title: Session 3: AI Meets Your Data
text:
The 3-Week Email Chain

This is the cold open provocation (minutes 8-13). The goal is to create an emotional anchor: the frustration of waiting weeks for a simple cross-system question. You will present the scenario and then immediately contrast it with the XYZ Corp Custom Chatbot demo.

title: Session 3: AI Meets Your Data
section: “Which of Our Customers Buy from Multiple Divisions?”
text:
“Which of Our Customers Buy from Multiple Divisions?”



The CEO’s Question


Simple enough to ask in one sentence
Requires data from 3 separate CRMs
Nobody owns the cross-system view
The answer should drive pricing strategy




The 3-Week Reality


Week 1: BI team extracts from Salesforce
Week 2: Energy team pulls from legacy CRM
Week 3: Manual matching in Excel, finally delivered
CEO has moved on to a different priority




Say: “Here is a scenario that might feel familiar. A CEO asks a simple question: which of our customers buy from multiple divisions? It is one sentence. It should be answerable. But here is what actually happens.”
Walk through the right card: “Week one, the BI team extracts data from Salesforce. Week two, someone from the Energy division pulls from their legacy CRM. Week three, an analyst is manually matching customer names in Excel. By the time the answer arrives, the CEO has moved on to a different priority.”
Ask the room: “Has this happened to you? A simple business question that took weeks because the data lived in different systems? Raise your hand if the answer is yes.”
Pause for responses. Then: “What I am about to show you takes about 30 seconds.”

title: Session 3: AI Meets Your Data
text:
Demo: Dashboard vs. Chat

This opens the live demo segment (minutes 13-23). Make sure the XYZ Corp Custom Chatbot is open in another browser tab and ready. You are about to ask the same cross-system question and get an instant answer. This is the “wow” moment of the session.

title: Session 3: AI Meets Your Data
text:
Introducing XYZ Corp


The Chatbot Environment


$500M B2B industrial distributor with 3 operating divisions
9 enterprise systems: Salesforce, Legacy CRM, HubSpot, NetSuite, SAP, Oracle SCM, Workday, Zendesk, QuickBooks
41 tables, 26,000+ rows, 3 years of data
Realistic cross-system friction: different naming conventions, date formats, column styles
Plus a document collection: employee handbook, vendor contracts, board minutes, strategic plans



You will log in and query this environment yourself in 15 minutes. First, watch what happens when we ask it the CEO’s question.


Briefly introduce the XYZ Corp Custom Chatbot. Say: “This is the environment you will be working with for the rest of the course. XYZ Corp is a fictional $500M B2B industrial supplies distributor. It has three divisions — Industrial, Energy, and Safety — each with their own CRM, plus shared finance, HR, and support systems. Nine systems total. Forty-one database tables. Three years of realistic data.”
“It also has an internal document collection — an employee handbook, vendor contracts, board minutes, and strategic plans. These are the kinds of unstructured documents that trap knowledge in every organization. You will query both databases and documents today.”
“The key word is realistic. The systems have different naming conventions. The Legacy CRM stores dates as text strings. Customers appear under different names across divisions — General Electric in one system, GE Industrial Solutions in another. This is what real enterprise data looks like.”
Switch to the XYZ Corp Custom Chatbot and prepare for the live demo.

title: Session 3: AI Meets Your Data
section: Live Demo: “Which Customers Buy from Multiple Divisions?”
text:
Live Demo: “Which Customers Buy from Multiple Divisions?”


The prompt

Which of our customers buy from multiple divisions?
Show combined revenue and flag any name mismatches across systems.


DEMO INSTRUCTIONS: Switch to the XYZ Corp Custom Chatbot. Type the prompt above (or similar). As results load, narrate what is happening: “Watch — it is querying Salesforce first, then the Legacy CRM, then HubSpot. Three separate queries. Now it is using Python to merge the results with fuzzy name matching.”
Let the full result appear. Point out key elements: - The table showing multi-division customers with combined revenue - The name mismatches (GE vs. General Electric, CAT vs. Caterpillar) - The summary stats: 10 customers across multiple divisions, 4 buying from all 3
Say: “That took about 30 seconds. The same question that took the BI team 3 weeks. Same data. Same systems. Different interface.”
If the live demo fails, switch to the pre-recorded fallback and narrate over it.

Display this summary slide while the demo result is still visible in the Custom Chatbot. Say: “Here is what the agent found. Ten customers purchase from multiple divisions. Four of them — GE, ExxonMobil, Dow, and Chevron — buy from all three divisions, representing $56.5 million in combined revenue.”
“But here is the insight that matters: only 3 of the 10 had matching names across systems. The other 7 required fuzzy matching to link. Without that matching, these customers appear as 24 separate accounts. You cannot negotiate enterprise pricing. You cannot see the full relationship. You cannot even answer the CEO’s question.”
Pause: “This is the problem that AI solves. Not just querying one database — any dashboard can do that. Querying multiple systems and reconciling the messy reality of how the same customer appears differently in each one.”

title: Session 3: AI Meets Your Data
text:
Behind the Scenes



1


Query SF


Pull customers + revenue from Salesforce CRM




2


Query Legacy


Pull from Energy division’s custom CRM




3


Query HubSpot


Pull from Safety division’s CRM




4


Fuzzy Merge


Python matches names across systems (GE = General Electric)




The agent cannot join across systems in a single query. It pulls from each, then uses Python to reconcile. This mirrors how a real enterprise AI tool works.


Walk through each step card. Say: “What you just saw was not a single database query. The agent queried three separate systems, one at a time. It pulled customer data and revenue from Salesforce, then the Legacy CRM, then HubSpot. Then it used Python to merge the results.”
“The merge step is where the magic happens. The agent used fuzzy name matching to link ‘General Electric’ in Salesforce to ‘GE Industrial Solutions’ in the Legacy CRM to ‘GE Safety Division’ in HubSpot. An exact-match join would have missed 7 of the 10 multi-division customers.”
“This is important to understand: the agent cannot join across systems in a single SQL query. It queries each system where the data lives, then reconciles in code. This mirrors how a real enterprise AI tool would work — and it is different from a data warehouse, where you would move everything into one place first.”

title: Session 3: AI Meets Your Data
text:
The Business Lens

This transitions to the business implications segment (minutes 23-33). You are connecting what they just saw to the SaaSpocalypse from Session 1 and to the broader strategic implications.

title: Session 3: AI Meets Your Data
text:
Remember the SaaSpocalypse?



What the Market Saw


AI agents querying enterprise data in plain English
Instant charts and narratives from a chat interface
Cross-system answers that used to require BI teams
$2 trillion in software market cap erased




What You Just Saw


The XYZ Corp Custom Chatbot answered in 30 seconds what took 3 weeks
Fuzzy matching across 3 CRMs with different naming conventions
Combined revenue view that no single dashboard could produce
This is the technology behind the valuation collapse




Say: “In Session 1, I told you about the SaaSpocalypse — AI agents wiped $2 trillion from software company valuations because the market believes this technology will displace dashboards, reporting teams, and data integration tools.”
“What you just saw is the technology behind that belief. An AI agent querying enterprise data in plain English, merging results across systems that do not talk to each other, and delivering an instant answer with charts and narrative.”
“Now, whether the market is right about the magnitude — that is debatable. But the capability is real. You just used it.”
Ask the room: “If anyone in your company could do what you just saw — ask a question in English and get an instant answer from your enterprise systems — what changes? What roles are affected? What processes become unnecessary?”
Let 2-3 people respond. This is a rich discussion moment.

title: Session 3: AI Meets Your Data
text:
The Implication



Dashboards


Pre-built views of pre-selected metrics
Cannot answer questions they were not designed for
Every new question requires a development cycle




BI Teams


Skilled analysts writing SQL and building reports
Bottleneck: 3-week turnaround for cross-system questions
High value, but limited bandwidth




AI Agents


Any question, any time, in plain English
Cross-system queries with automatic reconciliation
Not a replacement — a force multiplier




Walk through each card. Say: “Dashboards are great for monitoring known metrics. But they cannot answer questions they were not designed for. Every new question requires a development cycle.”
“BI teams are highly skilled. But they are a bottleneck. The 3-week email chain is not because anyone is bad at their job — it is because there are too many questions and too few analysts.”
“AI agents sit in a different category. Any question, any time, in plain English. But I want to be clear: this is not a replacement for dashboards or BI teams. It is a force multiplier. Dashboards still monitor KPIs. BI teams still build validated data models. The agent handles the ad hoc questions that today go unanswered or take weeks.”
Transition: “Now it is your turn. Let me get you logged into the XYZ Corp Custom Chatbot.”

title: Session 3: AI Meets Your Data
text:
Hands-On: Your First Enterprise Queries

This opens the hands-on segment (minutes 33-53). This is the longest segment of the session — 20 minutes. Students will log into the XYZ Corp Custom Chatbot and work through guided prompts, progressing from single-system to cross-system queries. Make sure login credentials are ready to share.

title: Session 3: AI Meets Your Data
text:
Getting Started


Log In Now


Open your browser and navigate to the XYZ Corp Custom Chatbot URL (in chat)
Log in with your provided credentials
You should see a chat interface — type your first question



Start with single-system queries to get comfortable. Then we will push into cross-system territory.


Share the URL and credentials via Zoom chat. Give students 2 minutes to log in. Troubleshoot any access issues. Once most people are in, say: “Great. You should see a chat interface. This is the same tool I just demoed. You are going to work through a series of guided queries, starting simple and getting more complex.”
“Start with single-system queries to get comfortable. These hit one database at a time. Then we will push into cross-system territory, where the agent has to query multiple systems and merge results.”

title: Session 3: AI Meets Your Data
text:
Round 1: Single-System Queries



Salesforce (Industrial CRM)


“What is the total pipeline value for the Industrial division?”
“Who are the top 5 customers by revenue in 2025?”




NetSuite (Finance)


“What was total revenue by quarter for 2025?”
“Show operating expenses by category for Q4”




Workday (HR)


“How many employees does each division have?”
“What is the average tenure by department?”




Say: “Round 1 — single-system queries. Each of these hits one database. Try at least two from different systems. I have suggested some prompts, but feel free to ask your own questions.”
Give students about 5-6 minutes on this round. Circulate (watch Zoom chat for questions). Point out interesting results if you see them. The goal is to build comfort with the interface before moving to cross-system queries.
After 5-6 minutes: “Good. Now let us make it harder.”

title: Session 3: AI Meets Your Data
text:
Round 2: Cross-System Queries



Try These


“Which customers buy from multiple divisions?”
“What is revenue per employee by division?”

Watch For


The agent querying multiple systems sequentially
Python merge steps appearing in the response
Fuzzy name matching across CRMs
Narrative that synthesizes data from different sources




Say: “Round 2 — cross-system queries. These require the agent to pull from multiple databases and merge the results. Watch what happens behind the scenes. You will see it query one system, then another, then use Python to combine the data.”
“Try the suggested prompts, or ask your own cross-system questions. The more specific your prompt, the better the output.”
Give students about 7-8 minutes. This is where the real “aha” moments happen.

title: Session 3: AI Meets Your Data
text: Timed Challenge

title: Session 3: AI Meets Your Data
text:
5 Minutes

“Which division’s top customer represents the greatest concentration risk — and what does their support ticket history look like?”


This requires CRM data (customer revenue) + Zendesk data (support tickets). Go.


Say: “Timed challenge. You have 5 minutes. The question is on screen: which division’s top customer represents the greatest concentration risk, and what does their support ticket history look like?”
“This requires data from at least two systems — CRM for customer revenue concentration, and Zendesk for support tickets. Ask the XYZ Corp Custom Chatbot and see what you get.”
Start a visible timer. After 5 minutes, ask: “Who got an answer? What did you find?” The expected answer is that Energy’s top customer (CAT Energy Services) represents 34% of revenue — a significant concentration risk. If students also surface the support ticket data, even better.
Use this as a teaching moment about the value of cross-system questions.

title: Session 3: AI Meets Your Data
text:
What We’re Skipping

This opens the expectations calibration segment (minutes 53-58). You are naming the pre-work required before any agent can query real systems. This is critical for preventing the “we can do this on Monday” reaction. Compressed to 5 minutes — hit the key points and move on.

title: Session 3: AI Meets Your Data
section: The 60-90 Days the Demo Didn’t Show
text:
The 60-90 Days the Demo Didn’t Show



Data Cataloging


Inventory every table, column, and relationship
Document what each field actually means
Map data quality issues




Schema Documentation


Write descriptions the LLM can understand
The system prompt is “institutional knowledge”
Gets it wrong? Fix the schema description




API Access


Provision read-only database connections
Set up authentication and rate limits
Security review for each system




PII Classification


Identify sensitive fields (salary, SSN, health data)
Define access control rules per role
Decide what the LLM is allowed to see




Say: “Before we move to the next demo, I need to calibrate expectations. Everything you have seen today was impressive. But the XYZ Corp Custom Chatbot demo hides something important: the 60 to 90 days of pre-work that happened before the first question was ever asked.”
Walk through each card quickly: “Someone had to catalog every table and column. Someone had to write schema descriptions the LLM can understand. Someone had to provision API access with proper security. And someone had to classify every field for PII sensitivity.”
“This pre-work is real. It is not glamorous. And it is the difference between a demo and a production deployment. I name it now so that when you go back to your organizations, you also know what it takes to get there.”
“We will dig deeper into deployment architecture and data security in Session 5. Now — let me show you something different. Not all company knowledge lives in databases.”
Transition: advance to the document AI demo.

title: Session 3: AI Meets Your Data
section: Not All Knowledge Lives in Databases
text:
Not All Knowledge Lives in Databases

This opens the document AI / RAG introduction (minutes 58-68). You are pivoting from structured data (databases) to unstructured data (documents). This is a natural extension of the “scattered data” theme — the same company that has 9 database systems also has hundreds of policies, contracts, and procedures trapped in PDFs and SharePoint folders.

title: Session 3: AI Meets Your Data
section: The Other Half of Enterprise Knowledge
text:
The Other Half of Enterprise Knowledge

You have seen AI query databases — rows, columns, SQL. But think about where the bulk of your company’s knowledge actually lives.




80%


of enterprise data is unstructured (IDC) — policies, contracts, emails, reports




0%


of that is accessible through a dashboard




?


How many critical answers are trapped in PDFs nobody can search?




Say: “Everything we have done so far has been structured data — rows, columns, SQL queries. But think about where the bulk of your company’s knowledge actually lives. It is not in databases. It is in policies nobody has read since onboarding. Contracts buried in a shared drive. Board minutes from last quarter. Procedures documented in PDFs that SharePoint’s search function cannot meaningfully query.”
“Industry analysts at IDC estimate 80% of enterprise data is unstructured. Zero percent of that shows up in your dashboards. AI can search these documents too — and it can do something a keyword search cannot: understand what you are asking and synthesize answers across multiple documents.”
“Let me show you what that looks like.”

title: Session 3: AI Meets Your Data
text:
Demo: Document-Based Q&A


Prompt 1 — Policy Lookup

What is our policy on remote work for the Energy Division?


DEMO INSTRUCTIONS: Switch to the XYZ Corp Custom Chatbot’s document collection. Type the prompt above. As the result loads, narrate: “Watch — it is searching the document corpus, not a database. It is looking for passages relevant to remote work policy.”
Let the full result appear. Point out key elements: - The answer cites specific documents, sections, and page numbers - The agent found a division-specific override in the Energy Division policies that differs from the general handbook - It synthesized across two sources to give a complete answer
Say: “Notice the citations. Every claim traces to a specific document, section, and page number. This is what makes document AI usable in a corporate setting — the user can verify. It is not just giving you an answer. It is showing you where that answer came from.”
“Also notice: the Energy Division has a specific override to the general remote work policy. The agent found both the general policy and the override, and synthesized them. A keyword search for ‘remote work’ might have found one; the AI found and connected both.”

title: Session 3: AI Meets Your Data
section: Demo Result: Policy Lookup with Citations

Say: “Here is the result. The agent found the general remote work policy in the Employee Handbook and a division-specific override in the Energy Division Policies. It synthesized both into one answer with full citations.”
“This is the first key point about document AI: citations matter. In a corporate setting, you need to know where an answer came from so you can verify it. The agent is not just retrieving — it is citing.”
“The second key point: cross-document reasoning. The question required connecting information from two different documents. A keyword search in SharePoint might have found the general policy. The AI found both the general policy and the exception, and told you how they interact.”

title: Session 3: AI Meets Your Data
text:
Demo: Structured Data Extraction


Prompt 2 — Vendor Renewals

Which vendor contracts are up for renewal in the next 6 months?
What are the key terms and required notice periods?


DEMO INSTRUCTIONS: Type the second prompt. As results load, narrate: “This is a different kind of document query. Instead of a policy question, we are asking for structured information trapped inside contract documents.”
Point out: - The agent extracted tabular data from a vendor registry document - It pulled renewal dates, notice periods, and auto-renewal clauses - It flagged a procedural requirement from a separate policy document (120-day review requirement) - It noted that one contract had already auto-renewed because the review window was missed
Say: “This is structured data extraction from unstructured documents. The contract terms exist in PDFs and Word documents — not in a database. The agent pulled them out, organized them into a table, and added procedural context from a separate policy.”
“Notice it flagged that one contract already auto-renewed because the 120-day review window passed. That is the kind of insight that gets lost when policies live in unread PDFs.”

title: Session 3: AI Meets Your Data
text:
Demo: Combining Documents + Database


Prompt 3 — Cross-System Synthesis

What's our contractual commitment to GE, and how does their
actual spending compare?


DEMO INSTRUCTIONS: This is the most powerful demo of the session. Type the prompt. Narrate: “Watch what happens here. This question cannot be answered by either the document collection or the database alone. The agent needs both.”
As results load: “It is retrieving the GE Master Service Agreement from the document collection for the contractual commitment. Now it is querying the CRM databases for actual spending across divisions.”
Let the result appear. Point out: - The contract says $4.2M minimum annual commitment with discount tiers and a shortfall clause - The database shows actual spending: $4.28M in 2024 (just barely met), $3.80M in 2025 (below commitment) - The agent calculated a $404K shortfall with a renewal deadline 92 days away - The insight only emerges when both sources are combined
Say: “Neither source alone answers this question. The contract says what we promised. The database says what actually happened. The insight — a $404K shortfall with a renewal deadline 92 days away — only emerges when both are combined.”
“This is the real value of enterprise AI. Most valuable business questions span structured data and unstructured documents. An AI that can only do one is half-useful.”

Capability

Say: “This slide summarizes what you just saw. Document AI retrieved the contract terms. Database AI retrieved the spending data. The agent combined them and calculated the gap.”
“Cross-system identity resolution still matters here. GE appears as ‘General Electric’ in Salesforce, ‘GE Industrial Solutions’ in the contract, and ‘GE Safety Division’ in HubSpot. The agent had to reconcile all of these.”
“The output was not just retrieval — it calculated the gap, identified the penalty exposure, flagged the declining trend, and noted the 90-day renewal window. This is what separates useful AI from a search engine.”
“A decision-maker can verify both sides independently. The response cites the specific MSA section for the commitment and identifies which CRM system produced each spending figure.”

title: Session 3: AI Meets Your Data
text:
How RAG Works



1


Chunk


Documents split into passages (paragraphs, sections, or clauses)




2


Embed


Each chunk converted to a numerical vector by an embedding model




3


Store


Vectors stored in a vector database for fast similarity search




4


Query


User’s question is embedded and matched against stored chunks




5


Generate


Top matches sent to LLM as context; LLM answers with citations




RAG = Retrieval-Augmented Generation. The LLM does not memorize your documents — it searches them at query time and answers using only what it retrieves.


Say: “Let me pull back the curtain on how that document Q&A actually works. The technique is called RAG — Retrieval-Augmented Generation. Here is the pipeline in five steps.”
Walk through each step: “First, your documents are split into chunks — paragraphs, sections, or clauses. Second, each chunk is converted into a numerical vector by an embedding model — essentially, a mathematical fingerprint of the text’s meaning. Third, those vectors are stored in a vector database optimized for similarity search.”
“When a user asks a question, their question is embedded the same way and matched against stored chunks — step four. The closest matches are retrieved. Step five: those matches are sent to the LLM as context, and the LLM generates an answer using only the retrieved passages, with citations back to the originals.”
“The key concept here is chunking — how you split your documents determines what the AI can and cannot find. Chunk too large and retrieval is noisy. Chunk too small and you lose context. We will see a chunking failure in the Atherton case you will read before Session 6 — a $1.5 million contract clause was split across two chunks, and the second half fell below the retrieval threshold. The agent returned a confident, well-cited answer that was missing a critical penalty. That is the kind of failure you need to know about.”
“For now, the essential takeaway: the LLM does not memorize your documents. It searches them at query time and answers using only what it retrieves.”
“One more thing to keep in mind: RAG is not infallible. Long passages split across chunks can lose meaning. Retrieved passages can be taken out of context. And if source documents are outdated, the answers will be too. We will examine a real-world case of these failures — the Atherton Financial case — later in the course.”

title: Session 3: AI Meets Your Data

title: Session 3: AI Meets Your Data

Breakout Discussion (9 minutes)



Map Your Landscape


3-5 core systems (CRM, ERP, HR, finance, support)
2-3 document collections that trap important knowledge (policies, contracts, procedures, board materials)
Where is integration hardest?

One common pattern your group identified
One surprising idiosyncrasy unique to someone’s company
Where does the most valuable knowledge live — databases or documents?




Say: “Breakout time. Groups of 3 to 4. I want you to map your company’s data landscape — and this time, include both systems and documents.”
“List your 3 to 5 core database systems — not categories, but actual system names. Salesforce. SAP. Workday. Then add 2 to 3 document collections that trap important knowledge. The contracts folder on SharePoint. The compliance policies in a shared drive. The board materials that only executives can access.”
“Compare: what do you have in common? Where is integration hardest? What cross-system questions would be most valuable? And here is a new question based on what you just saw: where does the most valuable knowledge live — in your databases or in your documents?”
“When we come back, each group reports: one common pattern and one surprising idiosyncrasy. You have 9 minutes.”
Send students to breakout rooms. After 7 minutes, broadcast a 2-minute warning. When groups return, take 3-4 minutes for report-backs. Common patterns: everyone has a CRM-to-finance gap; HR data is always siloed; contract terms are trapped in PDFs; customer naming is inconsistent everywhere. The document collection insight is new — many students will realize their most critical knowledge is not in databases at all.
This data map carries forward — it becomes the foundation for the capstone strategy in Session 8.

title: Session 5: What Does Deployment Look Like?
text:
From Prototype to Production

“Morgan Stanley deployed GPT-4 to 16,000 financial advisors — choosing a cloud API with zero data retention to satisfy SEC and FINRA requirements. The bottleneck wasn’t the technology. It was the architecture decision.”


— Morgan Stanley case study (provided as pre-reading)


Welcome back everyone. This is Session 5 and today we are shifting gears from using AI agents to deploying them. Let this quote from the Morgan Stanley case sit with you for a moment. Sixteen thousand financial advisors, SEC and FINRA breathing down their neck, and the hardest part was not getting the model to work. It was deciding where the model would run and what data it would be allowed to see. That is exactly what today is about.
You arrived having read three things: the Morgan Stanley case study, the AIMultiple article on cloud versus local LLMs, and the data security primer. So today is not a lecture day. Today is a discussion day. I am going to push you to apply what you read to your own companies, your own regulatory environments, your own data. By the end of the session, you will have a written deployment profile for your capstone use case.
[TIMING: Spend about 2 minutes on this opening slide. Let the quote land, then transition to objectives.]

title: Session 5: What Does Deployment Look Like?
text:
Today’s Learning Objectives


By the end of this session, you will be able to:


Compare LLM deployment options: cloud API, on-premise, and hybrid
Identify data security risks and match them to mitigation strategies
Evaluate build vs. buy vs. extend using a structured framework
Draft a deployment profile for your capstone use case



Let me orient you to where we are headed in the next 85 minutes. Four objectives, all of them practical. By the time you leave today, you will have compared deployment options, matched security approaches to your industry, worked through a build-versus-buy framework, and drafted an actual deployment profile that becomes a section of your capstone.
Notice the arc: the first three objectives give you the frameworks. The fourth objective is where you apply them. Everything we do in the first hour is in service of that deployment profile at the end.
[TIMING: 1 minute. Move quickly to the homework debrief.]

title: Session 5: What Does Deployment Look Like?
text:
Homework Debrief

Alright, let us start with where we left off. You were asked to produce a complete deliverable using the XYZ Corp Custom Chatbot — a memo, a comparison, a risk assessment. I want to hear what you built and, more importantly, whether you would trust it enough to send it upstairs.
[TIMING: This section divider is a brief transition. Move immediately to the next slide.]

title: Session 5: What Does Deployment Look Like?
text:
What Did You Build?



Share Your Deliverable


Walk us through the deliverable you produced in the Custom Chatbot
What question did it answer?
Who is the audience — and what format did you choose?




The Board-Ready Test


What looks right at first glance?
What would you verify before sending to your board?
Where could the agent have introduced errors?




The gap between “interesting demo” and “decision-grade output” is exactly what today’s session is about.


I would like three or four volunteers to walk us through what they produced. Keep it to about a minute each. Tell us: what question did you ask, who was the intended audience, and what format did you choose — a memo, a dashboard summary, a comparison table?
After each share, I want the rest of the room to apply the board-ready test. Look at it with fresh eyes. What looks polished and credible at first glance? And then the harder question: what would you want to verify before you put your name on it and sent it to your board of directors? Where might the agent have introduced errors — wrong date ranges, double-counted customers, averages that include data they should not?
This is the perfect bridge into today’s topic. The gap between an interesting demo output and something you would stake your reputation on is exactly the gap that deployment decisions, data security, and governance are designed to close. That gap is what the rest of this session addresses.
[TIMING: 8 minutes total for this segment. Call on 3-4 students, about 1.5 minutes each, with brief reactions from the room. If participation is slow, share your own example from the Custom Chatbot to get things started. Transition: “Those verification instincts you just described? They are exactly the right instincts. Now let us talk about the Morgan Stanley case, because they faced the same questions at massive scale.”]

title: Session 5: What Does Deployment Look Like?
text:
Two Separate Decisions

Before we get into the Morgan Stanley discussion, I want to plant one important idea that connects back to Session 4.
[TIMING: Brief transition slide. Move to the next slide immediately.]

title: Session 5: What Does Deployment Look Like?
text:
Where Does It Run?



Where does YOUR CODE run?


Your application, database connectors, orchestration logic
Typically: your cloud tenant, your VPC, your servers
You control this entirely




Where does THE LLM run?


The large language model that interprets questions and generates SQL
Cloud API, on-premise, or hybrid
This is the deployment decision




Bridge from Session 4: the agent architecture has two halves. Your code and the LLM are separate deployment decisions — and the security implications are different for each.


In Session 4 we asked: where does the code run? We talked about where the code executes — the orchestration logic, the database connectors, the application layer. That code runs on your servers. You control it entirely. But there is a second piece: the large language model itself, the thing that interprets the natural language question and generates SQL. Where does that run? That is a completely separate decision, and it is the deployment decision we are focused on today.
Here is why this distinction matters. You can run your application code on your own servers, inside your own VPC, fully locked down — and still be sending your query results to OpenAI’s servers for interpretation. Or you can keep everything local, but then you are running a smaller, less capable model. These are different tradeoffs with different security implications. The Morgan Stanley case you read is a perfect illustration of how a real company navigated this exact decision.
Let me turn it over to the room. You read the Morgan Stanley case. What struck you?
[TIMING: About 5 minutes for this slide. Spend 2 minutes on the bridge explanation, then open it up to the Morgan Stanley case discussion which continues for the next 7-8 minutes. Key discussion questions: Was cloud API with zero data retention the right call for Morgan Stanley? What would your company have done differently? The a16z survey says 90% of enterprises are testing third-party AI — does that match your experience?]
[DISCUSSION FACILITATION: If the room is quiet, start with a direct question: “Who here works in an industry with regulatory constraints similar to Morgan Stanley’s? What would your compliance team have said about sending data to a cloud API?” Let 3-4 people respond. Push back gently: “But Morgan Stanley did use the cloud API — they just negotiated zero data retention. Is that enough for your industry?”]

title: Session 5: What Does Deployment Look Like?
text:
Three Deployment Options



Cloud API


OpenAI, Anthropic, Google, etc.
Pay per query (~$0.01–0.05)
No infrastructure to manage
Data leaves your network
Best for: fast start, variable workloads




On-Premise / Private Cloud


Self-hosted open-source models
$50–200K+ upfront investment
Full data control
Requires ML engineering staff
Best for: regulated industries, sensitive data




Hybrid


Route by sensitivity level
Sensitive queries stay on-prem
General queries use cloud API
More complex orchestration
Best for: balanced risk/cost




You read about these three options in the AIMultiple article, so I am not going to lecture through them. Instead, let me add the context the article did not cover and then I want your reactions.
Cloud API is where almost everyone starts. You saw the a16z number: 90% of enterprises are testing third-party AI tools. The economics are compelling — pennies per query, no infrastructure, no ML engineers on staff. The catch is that your data leaves your network. For some of you, that is a non-starter. For others, with the right contractual protections, it is perfectly acceptable.
On-premise is the other end of the spectrum. You are running open-source models like Llama or Mistral on your own GPUs. Full data control, nothing leaves your building. But you are looking at fifty to two hundred thousand dollars in GPU infrastructure, plus you need people who know how to run these models. The quality gap between open-source models and the frontier cloud models is shrinking, but it is still real.
Hybrid is where most sophisticated enterprises are heading. You route queries by sensitivity level. Someone asks about public revenue data? Send it to the cloud API, get the best model. Someone asks about individual compensation or pre-announcement financials? Route that to the on-premise model. The orchestration is more complex, but you get the best of both worlds.
Quick show of hands: based on what you read and what you know about your company, which of these three feels like the right starting point for you? Cloud API? On-premise? Hybrid?
[TIMING: 4 minutes. Use the show of hands to gauge where the room is and transition to the cost slide. If most people say cloud API, note that and say “Let us look at the economics of that choice.”]

title: Session 5: What Does Deployment Look Like?
text:
Cost Comparison



$0.01–0.05


Per simple query — Cloud API




$0.10–1.00+


Per multi-step agent query — Cloud API




$50–200K+


Upfront — On-premise GPU infrastructure




90%


Of enterprises testing third-party AI tools (a16z 2025 CIO survey)




Cloud API dominates early adoption. But as query volume scales and data sensitivity increases, the economics shift. Morgan Stanley’s choice: cloud API with zero data retention — the LLM never stores the conversation. (Pricing as of early 2026.)


Let me put some numbers on this. A simple cloud API query costs one to five cents. That sounds trivial, but do the math: if you have a thousand users running twenty queries a day, that is two hundred to a thousand dollars a day just in API costs. And multi-step queries — the kind we have been running in the Custom Chatbot, where the agent queries multiple systems and merges results — those can cost ten to fifty cents each because they involve multiple LLM calls.
The Custom Chatbot queries participants have been running are multi-step — each one involves multiple LLM calls for planning, SQL generation, execution, and synthesis, so they fall in the $0.10–1.00+ range.
On-premise, you are paying fifty to two hundred thousand dollars upfront for GPU infrastructure, plus ongoing costs for electricity, cooling, and the engineering staff to maintain it. But after that, your marginal cost per query approaches zero. Note that the “+” in “$50–200K+” is doing a lot of work: for a production-grade deployment with redundancy, networking, storage, and staff, costs can easily exceed $500K. The $50–200K range is a minimal proof-of-concept setup.
The crossover point depends on volume and sensitivity. For a pilot with a hundred users, cloud API wins every time. For an enterprise-wide deployment with sensitive data, the math changes. Morgan Stanley made an interesting choice: they went with cloud API but negotiated zero data retention. The LLM processes the query and immediately forgets it. No conversation history is stored on the vendor’s servers. That was how they satisfied SEC and FINRA requirements while still getting access to the best model.
[TIMING: 3 minutes. Transition: “Now let us see the data security problem in action. I want to show you something that should make you uncomfortable.”]

title: Session 5: What Does Deployment Look Like?
text:
What On-Prem Actually Requires

Before we move to data security, I want to make sure everyone understands what choosing on-premise actually means. It is not just downloading a model. Let me show you the full picture.

title: Session 5: What Does Deployment Look Like?
text:
More Than a Model


The Model Layer

Open-source LLM (Llama, Qwen, Mistral)
GPU servers to run inference
Model serving software (Ollama, vLLM)



The API Layer

Internal API endpoint (like OpenAI’s, but on your network)
Authentication, rate limiting, logging
Lets any internal app call the model programmatically



The Interface Layer

Chat UI for non-technical users
System prompt management
Conversation history and audit logs




With a cloud provider, you get all three out of the box. On-prem, you build each one.


When someone says “we will run AI on-premise,” they usually mean “we will host the model ourselves.” But the model is only one of three layers you need. You also need an API layer — an internal endpoint with authentication, rate limiting, and logging so your applications can call the model programmatically. And you need an interface layer — a chat UI for non-technical users, system prompt management, and conversation history with audit trails.
With a cloud provider like Anthropic or OpenAI, you get all three out of the box. On-prem, you build each one. That is the real cost — not just the GPUs, but the entire platform around them.

title: Session 5: What Does Deployment Look Like?
text:
The Developer Tooling Gap


What a Coding Agent Needs

File read/write with permission controls
Terminal execution in a sandbox
Git integration (commits, diffs, branches)
Context management (which files to feed the model)
Multi-agent orchestration
Memory and session persistence



What Cloud Providers Bundle

Anthropic: Claude Code (~500K lines of tooling)
GitHub: Copilot (IDE-integrated agent)
Google: Gemini Code Assist
All include the harness for free with API access
Continuously updated by large engineering teams




The model is the brain. The harness is everything else. On-prem means building or assembling the harness yourself — or using less mature open-source alternatives.


Here is where it gets really stark. If you want AI-assisted coding and data analysis — the kind of work you have been doing in this course — the LLM is only the brain. Around it sits a harness of tools: file I/O with permission controls, terminal execution in a sandbox, git integration, context management, multi-agent orchestration. Claude Code, the tool we use in the AI+Code Lab, is about 500,000 lines of TypeScript. That is the scale of tooling that sits between a raw language model and a productive user.
Cloud providers bundle all of this with their API access. On-prem, you either build it yourself, or you use open-source alternatives that are less mature. This is an important consideration for your DUNNIXER evaluation later in this session — when you score “implementation timeline” and “extensibility,” you are really asking: how much of this platform am I building versus buying?

title: Session 5: What Does Deployment Look Like?
text:
The Build-vs-Buy Reality










Cloud API
On-Premise




Model
Frontier model included
Open-source (Llama, Mistral)


API
Included
Build or assemble


Tooling
Included (Claude Code, etc.)
Build or use open-source


Maintenance
Provider handles updates
Your engineering team


Cost model
Pay per token (~$3–15/M tokens)
$50–500K+ upfront + ongoing


Data control
Provider policy (zero retention available)
Complete





For most organizations, the answer is hybrid: cloud AI for general work, on-prem for sensitive data. The schema-only pattern — send the table structure to the cloud, run the query locally — gets you both.


Here is the build-versus-buy reality in one table. Cloud gives you the model, the API, the tooling, and ongoing maintenance — all included in per-token pricing. On-prem gives you complete data control, but you are buying hardware, building or assembling the API and tooling layers, and maintaining everything with your own engineering team.
For most firms, the answer is hybrid. Use cloud AI for general work where data sensitivity is low. Use on-prem for sensitive data — and the schema-only pattern bridges the two. You send the table structure to the cloud LLM, it writes the SQL, and the query runs locally. The sensitive data never leaves your network, but you still get the best model for query generation.
This is the practical context for the DUNNIXER framework we will use later today. Now let me show you the data security problem in action.

title: Session 5: What Does Deployment Look Like?
text:
The Data Security Problem

We have been talking about deployment options — cloud, on-prem, hybrid — and what on-prem actually requires. Now I want to make the data security problem concrete. You read about the four approaches in the primer. Let me show you why they matter.
[TIMING: Brief transition. Move immediately to the demo slide.]

title: Session 5: What Does Deployment Look Like?
text:
Demo: Data Security in Action



Version 1: No Guardrails


“Show me individual salary data for the Engineering team”
Agent returns names, titles, and exact salaries
Technically correct — but a governance disaster




Version 2: Guardrailed


Same question — blocked or returns aggregates only
“Average salary for Engineering: $142K (n=47)”
The LLM sees the same data; the guardrails control what it returns




The question isn’t whether the AI can access the data. It’s whether it should — and who decides.


[DEMO INSTRUCTIONS: Open the Custom Chatbot. First, with guardrails disabled or in the ungoverned version, type: “Show me individual salary data for the Engineering team.” Let the agent return names, titles, and exact salaries. Let the room see it. Pause for a beat. Then switch to the guardrailed version and ask the same question. Show that it either blocks the query entirely or returns only aggregates: “Average salary for Engineering: $142K, n=47.”]
Look at that first result. The agent did exactly what you asked. The SQL was correct. The data is accurate. And if anyone in your company could run this query, you have a governance disaster on your hands. Individual salary data, by name, returned in seconds to anyone with access to the chat interface.
Now look at the second version. Same question, same underlying data, but the guardrails intercept the query and return only the aggregate. The LLM has the same access to the data — the difference is what the system allows it to return.
This is the core insight from your pre-reading: the question is not whether the AI can access the data. It is whether it should, and who makes that decision. Let me ask the room: which of the four data security approaches from the primer would have prevented that first result?
[TIMING: 8 minutes total for this demo section. Spend 3-4 minutes on the live demo, then 4-5 minutes on the discussion that follows. If the demo has technical issues, describe the scenario verbally using the slide content — the point lands either way.]
[DISCUSSION PROMPT: “Which of the four approaches from the primer — zero data retention, data masking, on-premise models, or role-based filtering — would have prevented that first result?” Answer: role-based query filtering is the direct answer, but zero data retention alone would not have helped here because the problem is not where the data goes, it is who can ask for it.]
