Same English prompt. Same underlying data. The guardrails — not the model — determined whether this was a useful insight or a compliance violation.


Say: “Let me show you what actually came back in each version. On the left, without guardrails: full names, exact salaries, bonus targets. David Chen makes $210,000. Sarah Martinez makes $195,000. Anyone in the company with access to the chat interface can pull this in five seconds. That is a compliance disaster.”
“On the right, with guardrails: aggregate statistics only. The department has 47 people, the average salary is $142,000, the range is $98K to $210K. Still useful for planning and benchmarking, but no individual data exposed. And a clear policy reference telling you how to get individual records if you actually have authorization.”
“The model is the same. The data access is the same. The only difference is the guardrail layer that sits between the user’s question and the data. This is why the deployment decisions we discussed earlier matter so much. It is not about whether the AI can access the data. It is about the governance layer you build around it.”
Transition: “Let me formalize the four approaches to data security. You read about these in the primer.”

title: Session 5: What Does Deployment Look Like?
text:
Four Data Security Approaches









Approach
How It Works
Tradeoff




Zero Data Retention
LLM provider contractually deletes all inputs/outputs
Simplest; still sends data to third party


Data Masking / Anonymization
Strip PII before sending to LLM; re-identify on return
Preserves analytical value; adds latency


On-Premise Models
LLM runs inside your network; data never leaves
Full control; highest cost and complexity


Role-Based Query Filtering
Agent enforces permissions based on user identity
Granular control; requires identity integration





Most production deployments combine two or more of these approaches. Morgan Stanley used zero data retention + role-based access.


You read about these four approaches in the data security primer. Let me add what the primer could not cover: how these approaches combine in practice. Almost no production deployment uses just one of these. Morgan Stanley used zero data retention plus role-based access. A healthcare company might use on-premise models plus data masking plus role-based filtering — three layers.
Let me run a quick poll. Think about your company and the use case you have been developing. Which combination of these approaches would your compliance team require? I want you to think about this concretely, because you are going to need it for the breakout exercise.
Here is what I see most often: companies in regulated industries — finance, healthcare, government — start with on-premise or zero data retention as the baseline, then layer role-based filtering on top. Companies in less regulated industries — tech, retail, manufacturing — often start with cloud API plus zero data retention and add role-based filtering as they scale.
The key insight is that these are not mutually exclusive. They are a menu, and your compliance team will want to see a combination that addresses data in transit, data at rest, and data access. Does anyone have a specific scenario from their company where they can see this playing out? What would your compliance team insist on?
[TIMING: 5 minutes. This completes the data security section. Solicit 2-3 responses from the room. Transition: “Now let us shift to the other big decision. You know where to deploy and how to secure the data. The next question is: do you build this yourself, buy it from a vendor, or extend something you already have?”]

title: Session 5: What Does Deployment Look Like?
text:
Build vs. Buy

We have covered the “where does it run” question. Now the “who builds it” question. You may have read the DUNNIXER vendor scorecard with your Session 4 homework — if you did, you are ahead. If not, I will walk you through the framework now and we will apply it together.
[TIMING: Brief transition. Move to the next slide.]

title: Session 5: What Does Deployment Look Like?
text:
The DUNNIXER Framework


Eight Dimensions for Evaluating AI Solutions


Data integration — How well does it connect to your existing systems?
User experience — How intuitive is it for non-technical users?
Nuance & accuracy — How well does it handle domain-specific language?
Non-functional requirements — Security, compliance, scalability, uptime
Implementation timeline — Weeks, months, or quarters to production?
Xtensibility — Can you customize prompts, add tools, evolve it?
Economics — Total cost of ownership: licensing, infrastructure, and staffing
Risk & governance — Vendor lock-in, regulatory compliance, and auditability



DUNNIXER gives you a structured way to evaluate any AI solution, whether you are looking at a vendor product or evaluating whether to build internally. Eight dimensions, and every one of them matters.
Data integration is often the dealbreaker. If a vendor’s tool cannot connect to your Salesforce and your NetSuite and your legacy CRM, it does not matter how good the AI is. User experience determines adoption — if your sales team finds it easier to email the analyst than to use the AI tool, you have wasted your investment. Nuance and accuracy is about domain specificity: does it understand that “deal size” at your company means closed-won only? Non-functional requirements cover the boring but critical stuff: security certifications, uptime SLAs, compliance attestations. Implementation timeline is straightforward: can you get this live in weeks, or are you looking at a multi-quarter project? Extensibility is about the long game: can you customize the prompts, add new data sources, evolve the tool as your needs change, or are you locked into what the vendor ships? Economics forces you to look at total cost of ownership — not just the license fee, but infrastructure, staffing, and ongoing maintenance. And risk and governance covers vendor lock-in, regulatory compliance, and auditability — the questions your legal and compliance teams will ask before they sign off.
Let us apply this framework together. We are going to score a specific vendor product — Salesforce Agentforce — on these dimensions. But first, let me show you the four strategic options you are choosing between.
[TIMING: 5 minutes. Walk through each dimension with a brief concrete example. Transition to the four strategic options slide.]

title: Session 5: What Does Deployment Look Like?
text:
Four Strategic Options



Buy from Incumbent


Salesforce, Microsoft, SAP
Lowest risk, fastest start
Limited customization
You’re locked into their roadmap




AI-Native Startup


Purpose-built for AI-first workflows
Highest capability today
Vendor survival risk
May not integrate with legacy




Build Internally


Full control over every layer
Highest customization
Requires ML + engineering talent
Longest time to production




Hybrid / Extend


Buy a platform, customize on top
Example: Salesforce Agentforce
Moderate risk and control
Most common enterprise pattern




Four options, and most of you will land somewhere in the bottom right — the hybrid or extend approach. But let me walk through all four so you understand the tradeoffs.
Buying from your incumbent vendor — Salesforce, Microsoft, SAP — is the lowest risk path. They already have your data, they already have your security certifications, and their AI features are being bolted onto products you already use. The downside is limited customization. You get what they ship, on their timeline. If their AI copilot does not handle your specific workflow, you wait.
AI-native startups are purpose-built for AI-first workflows. They often have the highest capability today because AI is all they do. But there is vendor survival risk. Half of the AI startups that exist today will not exist in three years. And integration with your legacy systems may be nonexistent.
Building internally gives you full control. You saw in Session 4 that the core agent loop is fifty lines of code. But going from fifty lines to production is a different story — authentication, error handling, monitoring, audit logging. You need ML engineering talent, and you are looking at quarters, not weeks.
The hybrid or extend approach is the most common enterprise pattern. You buy a platform — Salesforce Agentforce, Microsoft Copilot Studio, something similar — and then you customize on top. You get the vendor’s infrastructure and security, but you add your own prompts, your own data connections, your own business logic.
Let me ask the room: where does your company typically land on build-versus-buy decisions for technology? Do you tend to buy from incumbents, or does your company have the engineering culture to build?
[TIMING: 5 minutes. Quick discussion to gauge the room’s inclinations before the Agentforce example.]

title: Session 5: What Does Deployment Look Like?
text:
Example: Salesforce Agentforce

What it is: Salesforce’s AI agent platform — extends your existing CRM with conversational AI, pre-built connectors, and guardrails.



Strengths

Already integrated with your Salesforce data
Enterprise security and compliance built in
Low-code customization for business users
Salesforce handles model updates and scaling



Limitations

Only works well within the Salesforce ecosystem
Limited cross-system querying (NetSuite, Workday, etc.)
Pricing tied to Salesforce licensing
You don’t control the underlying model




Agentforce illustrates the extend strategy: buy the platform, customize the prompts and tools, accept the vendor’s guardrails.


Let us score this together using the DUNNIXER framework. I will take us through two or three dimensions as a group exercise.
Data integration: Agentforce gets a high score within the Salesforce ecosystem. If your CRM data is in Salesforce, the connection is native. But how does it score for cross-system queries? If you also need NetSuite financial data or Workday HR data, Agentforce’s reach drops significantly. Think about the Custom Chatbot experience — we were querying across ten systems. Agentforce is strong within one.
Non-functional requirements: Salesforce already has SOC 2 certification, GDPR compliance, and enterprise-grade uptime SLAs. If your compliance team already trusts Salesforce with your data, the incremental trust required for Agentforce is small. That is a real advantage.
Extensibility: You can customize prompts and add tools through their low-code interface. But you do not control the underlying model. If Salesforce switches from one LLM provider to another, you go with them. That is the tradeoff of the extend strategy.
How would you score Agentforce on the remaining dimensions — user experience, nuance and accuracy, and implementation timeline? Anyone want to take a guess based on what you know?
[TIMING: 4 minutes. This is a group exercise. Solicit input from the room on the remaining DUNNIXER dimensions. Transition to the decision matrix.]

This matrix is your cheat sheet for the breakout exercise. Let me highlight the tensions.
Time to value versus customization: if you need something live in weeks, you are buying from an incumbent. But you are accepting low customization. If you need high customization, you are building internally, but you are looking at quarters to production.
Data control versus cost: full data control means building internally, which is the most expensive option. If you are willing to share data control with a vendor, you can cut costs significantly.
Risk is interesting because it comes in different flavors. Buying from an incumbent is lowest risk — they are not going anywhere, the product works, your compliance team already knows them. An AI-native startup carries vendor survival risk — will they exist in two years? Building internally carries execution risk — do you have the talent, and can you ship on time?
Look at the cost row. Year one costs range from thirty thousand dollars for a startup tool to over a million for an internal build. These are real numbers that your CFO will want to see. The hybrid approach typically lands in the hundred to three hundred thousand range, which is why it is the most common enterprise pattern.
You are going to use this matrix in the breakout. Keep it in mind.
[TIMING: 3 minutes. Walk through the key tensions quickly. This is reference material, not deep lecture.]

title: Session 5: What Does Deployment Look Like?
text:
What If You’re Not Ready?

Not every company is ready for an AI agent deployment. If you don’t have documented schemas, API access, or data catalogs, the honest answer may be: invest in data readiness first.



Signs You Should Wait


No one can describe your schema in plain English
API access requires a 6-month procurement process
Your data has no owner or steward
Compliance has never been asked about AI access to production data



The 60–90 days of pre-work from Session 3 applies here. Data readiness is the prerequisite that no vendor demo will mention.


I want to be honest with you about something that no vendor will tell you. Not every company is ready for this. If you are sitting there thinking “my company does not even have documented schemas” or “it would take six months just to get API access to our production databases,” then the honest answer for your capstone might be: the first phase of your AI strategy is data readiness, not AI deployment.
Remember Session 3, when we talked about the sixty to ninety days of pre-work? Cataloging your data, documenting your schemas, provisioning API access, classifying PII? That pre-work is not optional. It is the prerequisite. And if your company has not done it, that is actually a valuable finding for your capstone. Your deployment profile might say: “Phase 1 is data readiness, Phase 2 is a pilot deployment.”
There is no shame in that. In fact, a capstone that honestly says “we are not ready yet, and here is the six-month roadmap to get ready” is more credible than one that pretends you can deploy an AI agent next quarter when you cannot even describe your schema.
How many of you recognize your company in this list? Be honest. Show of hands. That is valuable self-awareness, and it should shape your deployment profile.
[TIMING: 3 minutes. Transition: “Alright, you have the frameworks. Now it is time to apply them. Let us move into the breakout.”]

title: Session 5: What Does Deployment Look Like?
text:
Breakout: Your Deployment Profile

This is the core exercise of the session. You have spent the last forty-five minutes absorbing deployment options, data security approaches, and the build-versus-buy framework. Now you apply all of it to your own use case.
[TIMING: Brief transition. Move to the breakout instructions.]

title: Session 5: What Does Deployment Look Like?
text:
Breakout Exercise


For Your Use Case (20 minutes)


Deployment: Which option — cloud API, on-premise, or hybrid? Why?
Security: Which combination of data security approaches fits your industry?
Build vs. Buy: Where does your organization land on the decision matrix?
Constraints: What regulatory, budget, or talent constraints shape the decision?



Each team presents a 2-minute summary. Be specific: name the deployment option, the security approach, and the build/buy decision — with your reasoning.


Here is how this works. You are going into breakout rooms of three to four people. You have twenty minutes. Each person should take about four minutes to present their use case and get feedback from the group. Use the four questions on the screen as your structure.
Be specific. I do not want to hear “we would probably use the cloud.” I want to hear “we would use cloud API with zero data retention because we are in manufacturing and our data is not regulated, but we would add role-based filtering because our HR data should not be visible to operations managers.” That level of specificity.
When you come back, each group presents a two-minute summary. I want you to report two things: what constraints were common across your group members, and where did industry differences lead to different answers? For example, did the healthcare person in your group land on a completely different deployment option than the retail person? Why?
Use the DUNNIXER dimensions to justify your build-versus-buy choice. If you say “we would buy from our incumbent,” tell me which DUNNIXER dimensions drove that decision. Was it data integration? Implementation timeline? Non-functional requirements?
I will drop into breakout rooms to listen in and answer questions. You have twenty minutes starting now.
[TIMING: 20 minutes in breakout rooms (minutes 58-72 of the session, with ~6 minutes for group reports when they return). Circulate among rooms. Listen for interesting tensions between group members. When groups return, call on 3-4 groups for 2-minute summaries. Highlight patterns: “I noticed three groups independently chose hybrid deployment — why do you think that is?”]
[AFTER BREAKOUT: When groups return, facilitate a 6-minute report-out. Ask each group: “What was the biggest disagreement in your group, and how did you resolve it?” This surfaces the real tensions executives face.]

title: Session 5: What Does Deployment Look Like?
text:
Individual Work: Capstone Deployment Profile


Write Your Deployment Profile (15 minutes)


Use case: One sentence describing your AI data agent
Deployment: Cloud API / On-prem / Hybrid — and why
Security: Which approaches and why
Build / Buy / Extend: Your recommendation with rationale
Key risk: The single biggest risk and your mitigation plan



This becomes a section of your capstone. Draft it now while the frameworks are fresh.


Now I want you to take everything from the discussion, the breakout, and the frameworks, and write it down individually. This is not a group exercise. This is your deployment profile for your capstone use case. You have fifteen minutes.
Five bullet points on the screen. Work through them in order. One sentence for the use case. Then your deployment choice with a rationale — not just “cloud API” but “cloud API because our data sensitivity is moderate and we lack on-premise GPU infrastructure.” Then your security approach — which combination of the four approaches and why. Then your build-versus-buy recommendation with a DUNNIXER-informed rationale. And finally, the single biggest risk and how you would mitigate it.
This becomes a section of your final capstone strategy document. You are not starting from scratch in Session 8 — you are building the capstone piece by piece. This is the deployment piece.
I will circulate and offer feedback. If you are stuck on any dimension, raise your hand. The most common place people get stuck is the risk and mitigation — so think about it from your compliance team’s perspective. What would keep them up at night?
[TIMING: 13 minutes of individual work (minutes 72-85). Circulate the room. Look for students who are stuck and offer targeted advice. Common issues: students who cannot choose between cloud and hybrid (push them to commit and justify), students whose “risk” is too generic (“data quality” — push them to be specific about what kind of data quality issue). At the 12-minute mark, give a 1-minute warning.]

title: Session 5: What Does Deployment Look Like?
text:
Before Next Session


Homework


For your identified use case: what governance requirements would your compliance team impose? Write down your top 3 concerns.
Think about who in your organization would need to approve an AI agent deployment — and what objections they’d raise.
Complete your deployment profile draft if you haven’t already.



Wednesday’s session: Can You Trust It? — we stress-test AI agents, learn the maker/checker framework, and design governance guardrails.


Three homework items, and they are all about governance — which is exactly where we are headed on Wednesday. First, put yourself in your compliance team’s shoes. For the use case you just drafted a deployment profile for, what governance requirements would they impose? Write down your top three concerns. These might be things like audit trails, access controls, data residency requirements, or regulatory-specific mandates.
Second, think about the approval chain. Who in your organization would need to sign off on an AI agent deployment? Your CIO? Your chief compliance officer? Your CISO? What objections would each of them raise? Write those down, because on Wednesday we are going to design governance frameworks that address exactly those objections.
Third, if you did not finish your deployment profile, complete it before Wednesday. You will need it.
Wednesday’s session is called “Can You Trust It?” and it is going to be a very different experience. We are going to deliberately break the Custom Chatbot. We are going to show you three failures that look like successes, teach you a verification framework, and then turn you loose to red-team the agent yourselves. Come ready to be skeptical.
[TIMING: 3 minutes. Be clear and concise on the homework. Transition to the closing.]

title: Session 5: What Does Deployment Look Like?
text:
Key Takeaway

Let me leave you with one thought.
[TIMING: Brief transition. Move to the final slide.]

title: Session 5: What Does Deployment Look Like?
section: The hardest part isn’t the model.
text: The hardest part isn’t the model.

title: Session 5: What Does Deployment Look Like?
section: It’s the decisions around the model.
text:
It’s the decisions around the model.

Where it runs, what it sees, and who controls it. Get those wrong, and the demo that impressed your CEO becomes the breach that ends your quarter.


The hardest part is not the model. It is the decisions around the model. Where it runs, what it sees, and who controls it. You worked through all three of those decisions today. You have a deployment option, a data security approach, and a build-versus-buy recommendation. Those are the decisions that separate a compelling demo from a production deployment.
Get those decisions wrong, and the demo that impressed your CEO becomes the breach that ends your quarter. Get them right, and you have the foundation for an AI capability that scales across your organization.
See you Wednesday. Come ready to break things.
[TIMING: 1 minute. End on a strong note. Total session time: approximately 85 minutes plus 5 minutes of buffer.]

title: Session 7: Let the Data Predict
text:
From “What Happened?” to “What Will Happen?”

Welcome back. Over the past six sessions you have learned to generate analyses with AI, query enterprise databases, build reporting pipelines, and evaluate reliability. All of that answers the question “what happened?” Today we add a fundamentally different capability: prediction. Instead of looking backward at your data, we ask it to look forward. By the end of this session, you will have built a predictive model yourself — using AI to handle the code — and you will know how to evaluate whether the predictions are trustworthy.
Before we get into prediction, let us debrief the Atherton case you read for homework. The governance failures in that case connect directly to what we are doing today.
[TIMING: 1 minute. This is the opening section divider. Move immediately to the Atherton debrief.]

title: Session 7: Let the Data Predict
text:
Atherton Case Debrief



Atherton’s Document AI Failures


Chunking failures — long contract clauses split mid-sentence, answers cited incomplete text
Access control gaps — junior analysts saw board-level compensation data
Stale documents — system answered from a superseded compliance policy




The Parallel to Predictive Models


Stale training data = stale predictions
Access control still matters — who sees predicted churn scores? Predicted salaries?
Garbage in, garbage out applies to ML just as much as to document AI




[ATHERTON DEBRIEF (8 minutes, minutes 0-8): Start by asking the room: “What was the most damaging failure in the Atherton case — chunking, access control, or staleness?” Call on 3-4 students. Push for specificity: “You said staleness — what specifically went wrong? What was the business impact of answering from a superseded policy?”
Listen for the themes: (1) Atherton did not version their documents, so the system could not distinguish current from superseded policies. (2) Access controls that existed in SharePoint did not carry over to the AI system. (3) Chunking is a technical problem that has business consequences — a contract clause split mid-sentence produces a wrong answer that looks right.
Then make the bridge: “Every one of these failures has a direct parallel in predictive modeling. If your training data is stale — say you trained a churn model on 2023 behavior but your pricing changed in 2024 — your predictions are answering from a superseded version of reality. If anyone in the organization can see predicted attrition scores for individual employees, you have the same access control problem Atherton had. And if your input data is messy — duplicate records, missing values, inconsistent formats — that is the ML equivalent of chunking failures. The garbage goes in, and confident-looking predictions come out.”]
[TIMING: 8 minutes total. Then bridge to prediction with: “With that context, let us talk about what prediction actually means in a business setting.”]

title: Session 7: Let the Data Predict
text:
Today’s Learning Objectives


By the end of this session, you will be able to:


Distinguish classification from regression and identify which applies to a business problem
Use AI to build and evaluate a gradient boosting model without writing code yourself
Interpret a confusion matrix, feature importance, and cross-validation scores
Apply the maker/checker framework to decide whether predictions are trustworthy



Four objectives today. First, you will learn the two types of prediction — classification and regression — and be able to categorize any business prediction problem. Second, you will use Claude to build a real predictive model. Third, you will learn to read the outputs that tell you whether the model is any good. And fourth, you will apply the same maker/checker framework from Session 2, but now to model evaluation instead of document verification.
The key insight for today: you do not need to understand the math. You need to understand what the model produces and how to evaluate it. AI handles the code; you handle the judgment.
[TIMING: 1 minute. Move to the two types of prediction.]

title: Session 7: Let the Data Predict
text:
Two Types of Prediction

Let us start with the most fundamental distinction in predictive modeling. Every business prediction falls into one of two categories, and knowing which one you are dealing with determines everything that follows — the model you build, the metrics you use, and how you interpret the results.
[TIMING: 15 seconds. Section divider — advance immediately.]

title: Session 7: Let the Data Predict
text:
Classification vs. Regression



Classification: “Which category?”


Will this customer churn? Yes / No
Is this transaction fraudulent? Fraud / Legitimate
Which segment does this lead belong to? Hot / Warm / Cold
Will this employee leave within 12 months?
Will this loan default?




Regression: “How much?”


How much will this customer spend next quarter? $12,400
What will demand be in March? 4,200 units
What price maximizes margin? $89.99
How many support tickets will we get next week?
What will Q3 revenue be?




Every business prediction is one of these two types. The type determines how you build the model and how you evaluate it.


Here is the fundamental split. Classification predicts categories — yes or no, fraud or legitimate, hot or warm or cold. Regression predicts continuous numbers — dollar amounts, quantities, prices.
The distinction matters because the evaluation is completely different. For classification, you care about things like: how often does the model say “churn” when the customer actually stays? That is a false positive, and it has a business cost — you might offer an unnecessary discount. For regression, you care about how far off the predictions are on average.
Let me give you a quick exercise. I am going to name five business questions, and I want you to tell me: classification or regression?
[AUDIENCE EXERCISE (2 minutes): Read each question, pause for hands or shout-outs: 1. “Will this prospect close?” — Classification 2. “What will this deal be worth?” — Regression 3. “Which customers are at risk of downgrading?” — Classification 4. “How many returns will we process in January?” — Regression 5. “Should we approve this credit application?” — Classification
After the exercise: “Good. Notice that the same business domain — say, sales — can have both types. ‘Will this deal close?’ is classification. ‘What will it be worth?’ is regression. The type depends on the question, not the domain.”]
[TIMING: 5 minutes total including the exercise. Move to gradient boosting.]

title: Session 7: Let the Data Predict
text:

This table shows six common prediction problems you will encounter in business. The left three are classification — they predict categories. The right three are regression — they predict numbers. Notice the pattern: classification problems tend to drive operational decisions (retain this customer, flag this transaction, prioritize this lead), while regression problems tend to drive planning decisions (how much to budget, how much to stock, what price to set).
For your breakout later, you will identify prediction problems at your own company. Use this table as a starting point, but do not limit yourself to these six.
[TIMING: 2 minutes. Move to gradient boosting.]

title: Session 7: Let the Data Predict
text:
Gradient Boosting: The One Method

We are not going to survey dozens of algorithms. There is one method that dominates tabular prediction — meaning prediction from spreadsheet-style data with rows and columns — and that is gradient boosting. This is what practitioners reach for first, what wins most Kaggle competitions on structured data, and what you will use today. Let me explain how it works conceptually.
[TIMING: 15 seconds. Section divider — advance immediately.]

title: Session 7: Let the Data Predict
text:
Why One Method?



70%+


of Kaggle competition winners on tabular data use gradient boosting




XGBoost


most popular implementation — used by every major tech company




#1


first choice for practitioners on real-world business data




Deep learning dominates images and language. For structured business data — the kind in your databases and spreadsheets — gradient boosting wins.


Why are we teaching you one method instead of ten? Because gradient boosting dominates. Over seventy percent of Kaggle competition winners on tabular data — meaning data that looks like a spreadsheet — use some form of gradient boosting. XGBoost, LightGBM, and CatBoost are the three most popular implementations, and they are used by every major tech company.
You have probably heard about deep learning and neural networks. Those dominate images, text, and language — which is why ChatGPT and Claude are so impressive. But for structured business data — rows and columns, the kind of data in your CRM, your ERP, your financial systems — gradient boosting consistently outperforms neural networks. It handles messy real-world data well: missing values, mixed data types, skewed distributions.
You do not need to master the implementation. Claude will write the code. But you need to understand the concept so you can evaluate vendor claims and ask the right questions when someone says “we built a machine learning model.”
This statistic is directionally correct for structured/tabular data competitions through the mid-2020s. The key point is that gradient boosting consistently outperforms other methods on the kind of spreadsheet and database data you work with.
[TIMING: 3 minutes. Move to the intuition slide.]

title: Session 7: Let the Data Predict
text:
How Gradient Boosting Works



🌳


Tree 1


Build a small decision tree. It gets some predictions right, some wrong.




🎯


Find Errors


Look at what Tree 1 got wrong. Focus on those mistakes.




🌳🌳


Tree 2


Build a new tree that specifically corrects Tree 1’s errors.




🔄


Repeat


Keep adding trees. Each one fixes the remaining errors. Stop when improvement plateaus.




The key insight: No single tree is smart. But hundreds of trees, each correcting the previous one’s mistakes, produce remarkably accurate predictions. This is ensemble learning — the wisdom of many weak learners.


Here is the conceptual explanation. Gradient boosting builds many small decision trees sequentially. The first tree makes predictions — some right, some wrong. The second tree does not start from scratch; it focuses specifically on the cases the first tree got wrong. The third tree corrects the remaining errors from tree two. And so on, for hundreds or thousands of trees.
No single tree is particularly smart. Each one is a “weak learner” — it might be only slightly better than random guessing. But the combination of hundreds of weak learners, each correcting the previous one’s mistakes, produces a model that is remarkably accurate. This is called ensemble learning.
Think of it like editing a document. The first draft has errors. A second pass catches some of those errors but might miss others. A third pass catches what the second missed. After enough passes, the document is polished. That is gradient boosting — many passes, each one cleaning up what the previous pass missed.
You do not need to remember any of this to use the technique. Claude will handle the implementation. But when a vendor tells you “we built a machine learning model for churn prediction,” you now know enough to ask: “What algorithm? Gradient boosting? How many trees? What was the cross-validation score?” Those questions signal that you are not someone who can be sold a black box.
[TIMING: 5 minutes. Move to the ML workflow.]

title: Session 7: Let the Data Predict
text:
What Is a Decision Tree?

A decision tree asks a series of yes/no questions about your data to reach a prediction:



Example: Will this customer churn?

Is monthly usage < 5 sessions? ├── YES → Has tenure < 12 months? │ ├── YES → CHURN (85% confidence) │ └── NO → Has open support ticket? │ ├── YES → CHURN (70% confidence) │ └── NO → STAY (60% confidence) └── NO → Has usage declined 3 months in a row? ├── YES → CHURN (65% confidence) └── NO → STAY (90% confidence)


Each tree in gradient boosting is small and simple — maybe 4-8 questions deep. The power comes from combining hundreds of these simple trees.


Before we move on, let me make decision trees concrete. A decision tree is just a series of yes/no questions. Is monthly usage less than five sessions? If yes, go left. Has the customer been with us less than a year? If yes, predict churn with eighty-five percent confidence.
Each individual tree in a gradient boosting model is deliberately kept small — maybe four to eight questions deep. This is by design. A deep, complex tree would memorize the training data instead of learning generalizable patterns. By keeping each tree simple and combining hundreds of them, gradient boosting gets the best of both worlds: complexity in aggregate, simplicity in each component.
You will see this in action in a few minutes when we build a model with Claude.
[TIMING: 2 minutes. Move to the ML workflow section.]

title: Session 7: Let the Data Predict
text:
The ML Workflow

Now let us walk through the end-to-end workflow for building a predictive model. This is the sequence you will follow in the hands-on exercise, and it is the same sequence that data scientists follow in production. The difference is that you will have Claude write the code while you focus on evaluating the output.
[TIMING: 15 seconds. Section divider — advance immediately.]

title: Session 7: Let the Data Predict
section: Five Steps to a Predictive Model
text:
Five Steps to a Predictive Model



📊


Prepare Data


Clean, explore, handle missing values




✂️


Split


Train set (80%) and test set (20%)




⚙️


Fit Model


Train gradient boosting on the training set




🔮


Predict


Apply model to the test set it has never seen




✅


Evaluate


Confusion matrix, accuracy, feature importance




Why split the data? If you evaluate a model on the same data it trained on, it is like grading a student on questions they already saw. The test set is the final exam with new questions.


Here is the five-step workflow. Step one: prepare the data — explore it, handle missing values, understand what each column means. Step two: split the data into a training set and a test set. The model learns from the training set and gets evaluated on the test set — data it has never seen. Step three: fit the model — this is where gradient boosting builds its hundreds of trees. Step four: predict — apply the trained model to the test set. Step five: evaluate — look at the confusion matrix, accuracy metrics, and feature importance.
The most important concept here is the train/test split. Why do we split the data? Because if you evaluate a model on the same data it trained on, you are measuring memorization, not generalization. It is like giving a student the final exam, then grading them on the exact same questions. Of course they will score well — they have seen the answers. The test set is a final exam with new questions. If the model performs well on data it has never seen, you can trust that it has learned real patterns, not just memorized the training data.
Cross-validation takes this further. Instead of one split, it does five or ten different splits and averages the results. This gives you a more reliable estimate of how the model will perform on truly new data. Claude will handle cross-validation automatically when you ask for it.
[TIMING: 3 minutes. Move to the simulated demo.]

title: Session 7: Let the Data Predict
section: Demo: Prompting Claude to Build a Model
text:
Demo: Prompting Claude to Build a Model


Step 1: Explore the Data

PROMPT: I have a CSV file with customer data. Each row is a customer. The columns include tenure, monthly_charges, total_charges, contract_type, payment_method, internet_service, and a column called “churn” that is Yes or No.
Please explore this data: how many rows, any missing values, and what does the distribution of churn look like?


[DEMO INSTRUCTIONS: This is a live demo. Open Claude and upload the customer churn CSV dataset. If the live demo fails, use these slides as the walkthrough and narrate what Claude would produce.
Start with this exact prompt. Claude will return a summary: number of rows, columns, missing values, and the class balance of the churn variable. Point out the class imbalance if it exists — typically 70-75% stay vs. 25-30% churn. Explain why this matters: if 75% of customers stay, a model that always predicts “stay” would be 75% accurate but completely useless.]
Let me walk you through what this looks like in practice. I am going to show you a conversation with Claude where we build a predictive model step by step. In the hands-on exercise, you will do this yourself.
Step one: give Claude context about your data and ask it to explore. Notice I am not writing any code. I am describing the data in plain English and asking Claude to do the exploration. This is the same maker/checker pattern from Session 2 — AI does the work, you evaluate the output.
[TIMING: 2 minutes on this slide. Move to Claude’s response.]

title: Session 7: Let the Data Predict
text:
Demo: Claude’s Exploration Output


Claude’s Response (simulated)

Here’s what I found in your dataset:
Dataset overview: - 7,043 customers, 21 columns - No missing values (after converting 11 blank TotalCharges to 0)
Churn distribution: - Did not churn: 5,174 (73.5%) - Churned: 1,869 (26.5%)
Key observations: - Month-to-month contracts churn at 42% vs. 11% for 2-year contracts - Customers with fiber optic internet churn at 42% vs. 25% overall - Average tenure for churned customers: 18 months vs. 38 months for retained customers
⚠️ The dataset is imbalanced (73/27 split). I’ll account for this when building the model.


Here is what Claude produces. It found 7,043 customers with 21 columns. No missing values after cleaning. And here is the critical finding: the data is imbalanced. Seventy-three percent of customers did not churn, twenty-seven percent did.
Why does imbalance matter? If I built a model that always predicts “no churn,” it would be seventy-three percent accurate. That sounds decent, but it is completely useless — it never identifies anyone who is actually at risk. Claude flags this and says it will account for it in the model. This is the kind of thing you need to watch for when evaluating model output.
Also notice the early insights: month-to-month contracts churn at forty-two percent, and tenure for churned customers is half that of retained customers. These are not predictions yet — they are patterns in the data that tell you the model has something to work with.
[TIMING: 2 minutes. Move to the model-building prompt.]

title: Session 7: Let the Data Predict
text:
Demo: Building the Model


Step 2: Build and Evaluate

PROMPT: Build a gradient boosting classifier to predict churn. Use an 80/20 train-test split and 5-fold cross-validation.
Show me: 1. The confusion matrix on the test set 2. Precision, recall, and F1 score 3. The top 10 most important features 4. Cross-validation scores
Explain the results in business terms, not just statistics.


Step two: ask Claude to build the model. Notice the specificity of this prompt. I am not just saying “build a model.” I am specifying the algorithm (gradient boosting), the split (80/20), the validation approach (5-fold cross-validation), and exactly what outputs I want. I am also asking Claude to explain results in business terms — not just statistics.
This is a skill you are developing: writing precise prompts that produce evaluable output. The more specific your request, the more useful the response. If you just say “predict churn,” Claude might use any algorithm and show you outputs you cannot interpret. By specifying what you want, you set yourself up to evaluate the results.
[TIMING: 1 minute. Move to Claude’s model output.]

title: Session 7: Let the Data Predict
text:
Demo: Model Results


Claude’s Model Output (simulated)

Model: GradientBoostingClassifier (200 trees, max depth 4)
Confusion Matrix (test set, 1,409 customers): Predicted Stay Predicted Churn Actual Stay 896 139 Actual Churn 102 272
Metrics: - Accuracy: 82.9% - Precision (churn): 66.2% — when we predict churn, we’re right 66% of the time - Recall (churn): 72.7% — we catch 73% of actual churners - F1 Score: 69.3%
Cross-validation: 81.4% ± 1.8% (stable across folds)


Here are the results. The model achieves 82.9 percent accuracy on the test set. But accuracy alone is not enough — remember, always predicting “stay” would give us 73.5 percent accuracy. We need to look deeper.
The confusion matrix is the key output. Let me walk through it. Top-left: 896 customers who actually stayed and the model correctly predicted “stay.” Top-right: 139 customers who actually stayed but the model predicted “churn” — these are false positives. Bottom-left: 102 customers who actually churned but the model predicted “stay” — these are false negatives, and they are the most dangerous. Bottom-right: 272 customers who actually churned and the model correctly caught them — true positives.
I will unpack the confusion matrix in detail on the next slide. For now, note the cross-validation score: 81.4 percent plus or minus 1.8 percent. This is stable across folds, which means the model is generalizing well, not just memorizing one particular train/test split.
[TIMING: 3 minutes. Move to the confusion matrix explanation.]

title: Session 7: Let the Data Predict
text:
Evaluating Models

Now that you have seen what a model produces, let us talk about how to evaluate it. This is where the maker/checker framework applies most directly. The AI built the model — now you need to decide whether to trust it.
[TIMING: 15 seconds. Section divider — advance immediately.]

title: Session 7: Let the Data Predict
text:
Reading a Confusion Matrix



What the Matrix Tells You


True Positives (272): Correctly predicted churn — these are your wins
True Negatives (896): Correctly predicted stay — no action needed
False Positives (139): Predicted churn, but customer stayed — unnecessary retention spend
False Negatives (102): Predicted stay, but customer churned — missed opportunity, the costly error




The Business Question


What is the cost of each error type?
False positive: you offer a discount to someone who was staying anyway (~$50/customer?)
False negative: you lose a customer worth $1,200/year in revenue
False negatives are 24x more costly — optimize for recall




The confusion matrix is not just a statistics table — it is a business decision tool. Let me show you why.
We have two types of errors. False positives: 139 customers the model flagged as likely to churn, but they were actually going to stay. What is the cost? You might offer them a retention discount unnecessarily — say fifty dollars each. That is about seven thousand dollars.
False negatives: 102 customers the model predicted would stay, but they actually churned. What is the cost? Each lost customer represents maybe twelve hundred dollars per year in revenue. That is a hundred and twenty-two thousand dollars in lost revenue.
False negatives are twenty-four times more costly than false positives in this example. That changes how you evaluate the model. You do not want the model with the highest accuracy — you want the model that catches the most churners, even if it means more false alarms. This is the precision-recall tradeoff, and it is a business decision, not a statistical one.
When you evaluate a model, the first question is not “what is the accuracy?” It is “what is the cost of each type of error?” That determines which metric matters most.
[TIMING: 4 minutes. Move to precision vs recall.]

title: Session 7: Let the Data Predict
section: Precision vs. Recall: A Business Tradeoff
text:
Precision vs. Recall: A Business Tradeoff



Precision (66.2%)


“When we predict churn, how often are we right?”
272 correct out of 411 churn predictions
Higher precision = fewer wasted retention offers
Optimize for precision when false positives are expensive
Example: fraud alerts that freeze customer accounts




Recall (72.7%)


“Of all actual churners, how many did we catch?”
272 caught out of 374 actual churners
Higher recall = fewer missed churners
Optimize for recall when false negatives are expensive
Example: cancer screening, churn prevention, fraud in high-value accounts




You cannot maximize both. Catching more churners (higher recall) means more false alarms (lower precision). The right balance depends on your business costs.


Precision and recall are inversely related — you cannot maximize both simultaneously. Increasing recall means casting a wider net, which catches more true churners but also flags more customers who were not going to churn. Increasing precision means being more selective, which reduces false alarms but misses more actual churners.
The right tradeoff depends entirely on your business context. For churn prevention, where a lost customer is worth far more than a wasted discount, you optimize for recall. For fraud detection where blocking a legitimate transaction angers a customer, you might balance more toward precision. For cancer screening, you overwhelmingly optimize for recall — you never want to miss a true positive.
This is a judgment call that AI cannot make for you. The model can give you any tradeoff you want by adjusting a threshold. Your job is deciding which tradeoff is right for the business.
[TIMING: 3 minutes. Move to regression metrics.]

title: Session 7: Let the Data Predict
text:
Regression Metrics: R² and RMSE

This section covers regression metrics for completeness. If you are running short on time, this can be skipped — today’s hands-on exercise uses classification only. Point students to these slides as reference material.




R² (R-squared)


“How much of the variation does the model explain?”
R² = 0.85 means the model explains 85% of variation in the target
R² = 1.0 is a perfect model (suspicious — probably data leakage)
R² = 0.0 means the model is no better than predicting the average
Business rule of thumb: R² > 0.7 is usually useful for planning




RMSE (Root Mean Squared Error)


“On average, how far off are the predictions?”
RMSE = $4,200 means predictions are off by ~$4,200 on average
In the same units as the target variable (dollars, units, etc.)
Ask: “Is this error margin acceptable for the business decision?”
RMSE of $4,200 on $50K deals is fine; on $8K deals, it is not




For regression problems — predicting continuous numbers — you use different metrics. R-squared tells you how much of the variation in the target your model explains. An R-squared of 0.85 means the model captures eighty-five percent of the variation in, say, quarterly revenue. The remaining fifteen percent is noise the model cannot explain.
RMSE — root mean squared error — tells you how far off the predictions are on average, in the same units as your target. If you are predicting deal values and the RMSE is four thousand two hundred dollars, that means the model’s predictions are off by about four thousand two hundred dollars on average.
The business question for RMSE is always: is this error margin acceptable for the decision I am making? An RMSE of four thousand two hundred dollars on fifty-thousand-dollar deals is fine for forecasting. The same RMSE on eight-thousand-dollar deals means the model is off by more than fifty percent on average — probably not useful.
You will not build a regression model in today’s exercise, but you will encounter these metrics when you or your team work on forecasting and demand planning.
[TIMING: 3 minutes. Move to feature importance.]

title: Session 7: Let the Data Predict
text:
Feature Importance: What Drives Predictions?


Top 10 Features (from our churn model)

Feature Importance ───────────────────────────────────── 1. contract_type 0.231 ← Month-to-month is the #1 risk 2. tenure 0.189 ← New customers churn more 3. monthly_charges 0.142 ← Higher bills → higher churn 4. internet_service_fiber 0.098 ← Fiber customers churn more 5. total_charges 0.074 6. payment_method_electronic 0.062 7. online_security_no 0.048 8. tech_support_no 0.041 9. online_backup_no 0.035 10. paperless_billing 0.028


The maker/checker question: Do these make business sense? Contract type and tenure as top predictors? Yes — that passes the smell test. If “customer_id” were the top feature, something is very wrong.


Feature importance tells you which variables the model relies on most. This is one of the most valuable outputs for business decision-making, separate from the predictions themselves.
Look at this list. Contract type is the number one predictor of churn. Month-to-month customers churn at much higher rates than those on annual or two-year contracts. Tenure is second — newer customers are more likely to leave. Monthly charges is third — customers paying higher bills are more sensitive to competitors.
Now apply the maker/checker framework. Do these features make business sense? Absolutely. If you asked any experienced sales or customer success leader “what drives churn?”, they would say contract type, tenure, and price sensitivity. The model confirms domain expertise.
But here is the red flag to watch for. If “customer_id” appeared as a top feature, that would mean the model is memorizing individual customers, not learning patterns. If “zip_code” appeared as number one, it might be exploiting a data artifact rather than a real business driver. Feature importance is your most powerful tool for catching models that are technically accurate but fundamentally flawed.
[TIMING: 3 minutes. Move to the maker/checker framework for models.]

title: Session 7: Let the Data Predict
text:
The Maker/Checker Framework for Models




Better Than Baseline?



Does the model beat a naive approach?
Baseline for churn: predict “stay” for everyone → 73.5% accuracy
Our model: 82.9% accuracy
9.4 percentage points better — real improvement





Features Make Sense?



Do the top predictors match domain knowledge?
Contract type, tenure, charges → makes sense
Customer ID, row number → data leakage
Ask a domain expert: “Do these surprise you?”





Generalizes Well?



Cross-validation: 81.4% ± 1.8%
Training accuracy: 85.2%
Small gap → model is generalizing
If train = 99% but CV = 60% → overfitting




Here is your three-question checklist for evaluating any predictive model. You do not need to understand the math. You need to ask these three questions.
Question one: does the model beat a baseline? The simplest baseline for churn is “predict everyone stays” — that gives you 73.5 percent accuracy for free. Our model gets 82.9 percent. That is a real improvement of 9.4 percentage points. If a model only beats the baseline by one or two points, it may not be worth the complexity.
Question two: do the important features make business sense? We already covered this. Contract type, tenure, and monthly charges as top predictors? That matches what any experienced manager would tell you. If the top features do not make sense, the model may be exploiting a data artifact.
Question three: does the model generalize? Compare the cross-validation score to the training score. Our model gets 85.2 percent on training data and 81.4 percent on cross-validation. That is a small gap, which means the model learned real patterns, not memorized the training data. If you saw training accuracy of 99 percent but cross-validation accuracy of 60 percent, that model is memorizing, not learning. It will fail on new data.
Three questions. That is your entire evaluation framework. AI builds the model — you ask these three questions.
[TIMING: 3 minutes. Move to red flags.]

title: Session 7: Let the Data Predict
section: Red Flags: When Not to Trust the Model
text:
Red Flags: When Not to Trust the Model



🚩 Too Good to Be True


99% accuracy usually means data leakage
The target variable leaked into the features
Example: including “cancellation_date” to predict churn




🚩 Nonsense Features


Customer ID, row number, or timestamp as top features
The model is memorizing, not learning
Always check feature importance before trusting predictions




🚩 Big Train/Test Gap


Train accuracy: 98%
Test accuracy: 65%
The model memorized training data and fails on new data




🚩 Unstable Cross-Val


Scores: 85%, 62%, 78%, 91%, 55%
High variance across folds
Model is sensitive to which data it sees — not reliable




Four red flags that should make you stop and investigate before deploying a model.
First: too good to be true. If you see 99 percent accuracy, do not celebrate — investigate. Almost always, this means data leakage: information about the outcome leaked into the input features. The classic example is including “cancellation_date” as a feature when predicting churn. Of course the model is perfect — it can see the answer in the input.
Second: nonsense features at the top of the importance list. Customer ID, row number, account creation timestamp — these should never be meaningful predictors. If they are, the model is memorizing specific customers rather than learning generalizable patterns.
Third: a large gap between training and test performance. If the model gets 98 percent on training data but 65 percent on test data, it has memorized the training set. It learned the noise, not the signal.
Fourth: unstable cross-validation scores. If the model gets 85 percent on one fold and 55 percent on another, it is highly sensitive to which data it sees. That is not a model you can rely on for business decisions.
Any of these red flags means you need to go back and investigate before trusting the predictions.
[TIMING: 3 minutes. Move to the hands-on exercise.]

title: Session 7: Let the Data Predict
text:
Five Guided Steps



1️⃣


Explore


“Explore this data. How many rows? Missing values? What’s the churn rate?”




2️⃣


Build


“Build a gradient boosting classifier with 80/20 split and cross-validation.”




3️⃣


Evaluate


“Show me the confusion matrix. What’s the false negative rate?”




4️⃣


Interpret


“What are the top 10 features? Do they make business sense?”




5️⃣


Challenge


“What would a skeptical VP push back on? How would you improve this?”




Bonus prompt: “If I could only act on the top 200 highest-risk customers, which ones would you flag and why?”


Here are your five steps. Step one: explore the data. Ask Claude to tell you what is in the dataset, how many rows, any missing values, and what the churn rate looks like. Step two: build the model. Ask for a gradient boosting classifier with an 80/20 train-test split and 5-fold cross-validation. Step three: evaluate. Ask for the confusion matrix and specifically the false negative rate — how many churners is the model missing? Step four: interpret the feature importance. Do the top features make sense? Step five: challenge the model. Ask Claude what a skeptical VP would push back on. This is the maker/checker step.
The bonus prompt is my favorite: “If I could only act on the top 200 highest-risk customers, which ones would you flag and why?” This forces the model output into a concrete business action. It is not about accuracy in the abstract — it is about which specific customers get a retention call.
You have twenty minutes. Go.
[TIMING: 18-20 minutes for the exercise. Circulate the room. Common issues: (1) Students who upload the CSV but do not describe it — remind them to give Claude context. (2) Students who get a model but do not evaluate it — push them to steps 3-5. (3) Students who get stuck on errors — usually a Claude timeout on large code blocks, just ask Claude to try again.]

title: Session 7: Let the Data Predict
text:
Example: Step 5 in Action


Prompt: Challenge the Model

PROMPT: What would a skeptical VP of Customer Success push back on if I presented this model? What are the weaknesses?



Claude’s Response (simulated)

A skeptical VP would likely raise these concerns:

“We’re missing 27% of churners.” The recall of 72.7% means 102 customers who churned were not flagged. If each lost customer costs $1,200/year, that’s $122K in missed revenue.
“Contract type is the top feature — we already know that.” The model confirms what the team already suspects. The VP wants to know: what does the model tell us that we DON’T already know? → Look at features 4-10 for non-obvious insights.
“This is trained on historical data.” If we just changed our pricing or launched a new product, past churn patterns may not predict future behavior. The model needs retraining regularly.
“What do we DO with the predictions?” A model is only useful if it drives action. What’s the intervention for flagged customers? Who owns the follow-up?



This is what step five looks like in practice. When you ask Claude to play the role of a skeptical VP, it surfaces exactly the right concerns. The recall gap means we are missing a quarter of churners. The top features confirm existing intuition rather than revealing new insights. The model is trained on historical data that may not reflect current conditions. And most importantly: what do you actually do with the predictions?
That fourth point is the most important. A model that predicts churn is only valuable if someone acts on the predictions. Who gets the list of at-risk customers? What is the intervention? Is it an email, a phone call, a discount offer? How do you measure whether the intervention worked? These are business questions, not technical ones. The model is the easy part. The organizational response is the hard part.
[TIMING: This slide is shown during the exercise wrap-up, around minute 55. Spend 2-3 minutes on it before moving to the debrief.]

Say: “Your model works. Now let’s make it usable. Open the AI+Code Lab — you should still have the model code from the previous exercise. Tell Claude Code to turn it into an interactive web app.”
“The prompt is on the slide. One sentence. Claude Code handles the rest — it picks the framework, builds the input form, the prediction logic, the visualization. The result is a working web app that anyone on your team can use without touching code or understanding the model.”
“Once you have it running, try the variation: add a batch mode that reads a CSV and flags all high-risk customers. This is the difference between a one-at-a-time tool and a production tool.”
[TIMING: 10 minutes. Walk the room. Common issues: (1) students whose model code was lost — tell them to rebuild it with one prompt, it takes 2 minutes. (2) Students who finish fast — challenge them to add the batch mode or to add recommended actions for each risk level. (3) Students who get a non-working app — paste the error back into Claude Code.
This exercise is the seed for the capstone prototype. Students who build a working prediction app here can extend it for Session 8. Make this explicit: “What you are building right now could become your capstone demo.”]

From raw data to a decision tool — and you built it yourself. This is what AI + code gives you: not just answers, but applications.


Say: “Look at what you just did. Data, model, app, decision. You started with a CSV file. You built a predictive model. You deployed it as a web app. And the result is a decision tool that a sales manager could use every morning to prioritize retention outreach.”
“You built the entire arc in one session. That is the point: with AI assistance, you can go from raw data to a deployable application in a single sitting. This same approach works for any prediction problem — demand forecasting, lead scoring, equipment failure, employee attrition.”
“When you think about your capstone, this is the level of specificity that scores well. Not ‘we will use ML’ but ‘we will build a churn prediction app that the customer success team uses every morning.’ And you can demo the working prototype.”

title: Session 7: Let the Data Predict
text:
Breakout: Prediction Opportunities

Time for the breakout. You have practiced building and evaluating a model on someone else’s data. Now I want you to think about your own organization. Where could prediction create value? What data would you need? And critically — what would you do with the predictions?

title: Session 7: Let the Data Predict
text:
Breakout Instructions


Breakout Exercise (5 minutes, groups of 3-4)

Each person shares in 1 minute:

What would you predict? (Be specific: “customer churn” not “make better predictions”)
What data would you need? Do you have it?
What would you DO with the predictions? Who acts on them?



Report back: Each group shares the most valuable prediction opportunity.


[BREAKOUT INSTRUCTIONS: Create breakout rooms of 3-4 people. Display this slide in the rooms. Each person shares one prediction problem in about a minute. The group picks the most valuable opportunity to share with the full class.
TIMING: 5 minutes in breakout rooms. Then 3 minutes for report-back — call on 3-4 groups. Push for specificity: “You said ‘predict demand’ — demand for what? What data would you use? What would you do differently if the model said demand would spike next month?”
This is shorter than previous breakouts because students will do this thinking more thoroughly for the capstone. The goal here is just to prime the pump.]

title: Session 7: Let the Data Predict

Most Valuable Predictions

Biggest Data Obstacles

Would need to combine multiple sources
Sound familiar from Sessions 3-4?




The Action Question


A prediction without action is a report
Who receives the predictions?
What decision changes?
This is the capstone question




[REPORT-BACK: Call on 3-4 groups. Capture the themes on screen or verbally. The three columns on this slide will likely match what you hear: valuable opportunities, data obstacles, and the action question.
Key teaching moment: emphasize the rightmost card. A prediction without action is just another report. The value of predictive modeling is not the model itself — it is the decision that changes because of the model. “We predict this customer will churn” is only valuable if someone calls that customer, offers a retention deal, or escalates to account management. “We predict demand will spike” is only valuable if someone adjusts inventory, staffing, or marketing spend.
This connects directly to the capstone. Students should be thinking: “In my capstone strategy, if I include predictive modeling, what specific decision does it improve and who acts on it?”
TIMING: 5 minutes for report-back. Then bridge to capstone.]

Capstone: Build It and Pitch It



Your Prototype


A working agent, skill, dashboard, or automated workflow
Built on the AI+Code Lab (or the XYZ Corp Chatbot)
Does not need to be production-ready — but it needs to run
Could be: the agent from Session 4, the prediction app from today, a new tool for your specific use case




Your Strategy + Pitch (5 minutes)


~2 min: demo your prototype — show what it does
~3 min: pitch your deployment plan — governance, business case, Day 1 action
Breakout groups vote on strongest strategy
5 winners present to full class




The capstone is not hypothetical. You demo something that works and explain how you would deploy it. The prototype grounds your strategy in reality.


Here is the capstone format — and it has changed from what you may have heard earlier. You are not just pitching a strategy document. You are demoing a working prototype and pitching the deployment plan around it.
Your prototype does not need to be production-ready. It needs to run. It could be the data agent you built in Session 4, extended and refined. It could be the prediction app you built today. It could be a new tool entirely — a skill, a dashboard, an automated report pipeline. The key is that it works and it addresses a real task at your organization.
Your presentation is five minutes. About two minutes showing the prototype — run it, show the output, explain what it does. About three minutes on the deployment plan — governance, business case, and a concrete Day 1 action.
The most important self-check: does your prototype actually run? And does your Day 1 action actually happen on Day 1?
[TIMING: 3 minutes. Move to homework summary.]

Three homework items. First and most important: build or refine your prototype. You have been building throughout this course — the skill from Session 2, the data agent from Session 4, the prediction app from today. Pick one and extend it, or build something new for your specific use case. It needs to run. A simple, working tool beats an ambitious, broken one.
Second, write your strategy document. Use the template and the rubric. The strategy should explain how you would deploy the prototype at scale — governance, business case, 90-day pilot.
Third, prepare your presentation. Five minutes total: about two minutes demoing the prototype, about three minutes on the strategy. Practice running the prototype so the demo is smooth — nothing undermines a pitch like a broken demo.
The optional viewing is Demos 6 and 7, which show building a RAG system and combining it with a database agent. Relevant if your capstone involves document AI.

title: Session 7: Let the Data Predict
text:
Key Takeaways

Let me leave you with three takeaways from today.
[TIMING: 15 seconds. Section divider — advance immediately.]

title: Session 7: Let the Data Predict
text:
Three Things to Remember



AI Builds the Model


You do not need to write code
You do not need to understand the math
You need to write clear prompts and evaluate the output




Three Questions to Ask


Better than baseline?
Features make business sense?
Generalizes to new data?
If yes to all three, proceed with caution




Predictions Need Action


A model without action is just a report
Who receives predictions?
What decision changes?
This is what separates strategy from spectacle




Three takeaways. First: AI builds the model, you evaluate it. You did this today — you prompted Claude, it wrote the code, and you evaluated the confusion matrix, feature importance, and cross-validation scores. That is the workflow.
Second: three questions. Does it beat the baseline? Do the features make sense? Does it generalize? If the answer to all three is yes, you have a model worth exploring further. If any answer is no, investigate before trusting the predictions.
Third: predictions need action. This is the most important takeaway. A churn model is only valuable if someone calls the at-risk customers. A demand forecast is only valuable if someone adjusts inventory. Your capstone should specify not just what AI does, but what humans do differently because of it.
See you Monday for your presentations. Come prepared — this is your chance to synthesize everything from the past seven sessions into a strategy you can actually take back to your organization.
[TIMING: 2 minutes. End of session.]

title: From BI to AI
text:
What This Course Is About

AI agents that understand plain English can now generate charts, query databases, write reports, build predictive models, and assemble complete deliverables — all from a single prompt.

This course teaches you to use these tools for:

Personal productivity — charts, documents, analyses, automated workflows
Enterprise data — querying databases, merging cross-system data, extracting information from documents
Organizational strategy — deployment decisions, governance, and AI adoption planning


Welcome to From BI to AI: Making Data-Driven Decisions with Agentic AI. I am Kerry Back, a professor of finance at Rice University. In this short video, I want to give you a preview of what we will cover over our four weeks together and introduce the two tools you will be using throughout the course.
This course is about a specific capability that has emerged in the past year: AI agents that can write and execute code on your behalf. You describe what you want in plain English, and the agent produces it — a chart, a financial analysis, an executive summary, a dashboard.
This is not about becoming a programmer. It is about learning to direct AI effectively and verify its output — including learning when AI gets it wrong and how to catch it.
The course uses Anthropic’s Claude as its primary AI tool. The skills you learn transfer to other AI platforms, but the exercises and environments are built on Claude.
We start with personal productivity, move to enterprise data, cover deployment and governance, and finish with predictive modeling and strategy.

title: From BI to AI
text:
The Course Arc

Enterprise Data & Build an Agent




🏗️
